# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic transition below is written out in full, with a text explanation between each step. Multi-line equations use a single `aligned` environment.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Min-Max Normalization (Range $[0,1]$)

**Objective:** Map a value $x$ from the interval $[\min, \max]$ to a new value $x' \in [0,1]$.

**Step 1 — Set up the linear mapping.**
Assume a linear relationship $x' = a x + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants chosen to satisfy the boundary conditions.

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary conditions.**
We require $x = \min \Rightarrow x' = 0$ and $x = \max \Rightarrow x' = 1$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the first condition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= a \cdot \min + b \\
\Rightarrow b &= -a \cdot \min
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the second condition.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 &= a \cdot \max + b \\
1 &= a \cdot \max - a \cdot \min \\
1 &= a (\max - \min) \\
\Rightarrow a &= \dfrac{1}{\max - \min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $a$ and $b$ back into the original mapping.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= \dfrac{1}{\max - \min} \cdot x - \dfrac{\min}{\max - \min} \\
x' &= \dfrac{x - \min}{\max - \min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $x' = \dfrac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}$ — the standard min-max normalization formula. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Z-Score Normalization (Mean $\mu$, Standard Deviation $\sigma$)

**Objective:** Transform a value $x$ into $z$ such that the new distribution has mean 0 and standard deviation 1.

**Step 1 — Start with the linear shift-and-scale model.**

$$
z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the new mean is 0.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[z] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma} \left(\mathbb{E}[x] - \mu\right) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma} (\mu - \mu) \\
&= 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the new variance is 1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(z) &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{1}{\sigma^2} \mathbb{E}\!\left[(x - \mu)^2\right] \\
&= \dfrac{\sigma^2}{\sigma^2} \\
&= 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ correctly standardizes the data. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Principal Component Analysis (PCA) from First Principles

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

We have a centered dataset $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ (each column has zero mean). We wish to find a unit vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^{p}$ ($\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert = 1$) onto which the projected data $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}$ has **maximum variance**.

### 3.3.2 Express the Variance of the Projection

The variance of the projected scalar values $\mathbf{z}$ is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\mathbf{z}) &= \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i^2 \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n} (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w})^{\top} (\mathbf{X}\mathbf{w}) \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w} \\
&= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \left(\dfrac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}\right) \mathbf{w} \\
&= \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ is the sample covariance matrix (note: $\mathbf{X}$ is already centered).

### 3.3.3 Constrained Optimization

We want to maximize $J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1$.

Using a Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1)
$$

### 3.3.4 Differentiate and Set to Zero

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} &= 2 \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} &= \lambda \mathbf{w}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely the **eigenvalue equation** of $\mathbf{C}$. Hence the optimal direction $\mathbf{w}$ must be an eigenvector of $\mathbf{C}$.

### 3.3.5 Determine the Maximum

Substituting $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ back into the objective:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(\mathbf{w}) &= \mathbf{w}^{\top} (\lambda \mathbf{w}) \\
&= \lambda \, \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} \\
&= \lambda \cdot 1 \\
&= \lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

To maximize $J(\mathbf{w})$, we pick the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$ and its corresponding eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$.

### 3.3.6 Subsequent Components

The second principal component $\mathbf{w}_2$ maximizes the variance subject to being **orthogonal** to $\mathbf{w}_1$ (i.e., $\mathbf{w}_1^{\top} \mathbf{w}_2 = 0$). By an identical Lagrange argument, $\mathbf{w}_2$ is the eigenvector corresponding to the second-largest eigenvalue $\lambda_2$. This pattern continues, yielding:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}_i = \lambda_i \mathbf{w}_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, p}
$$

### 3.3.7 Final Result

The first $k$ principal components are the eigenvectors $\mathbf{w}_1, \mathbf{w}_2, \dots, \mathbf{w}_k$ of the covariance matrix $\mathbf{C}$, ordered by descending eigenvalue. The transformed low-dimensional data is:

$$
\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}_k, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1 \mid \mathbf{w}_2 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf{w}_k]
$$

with $\mathbf{Z} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — PCA by Hand

**Given Data Matrix** (centered, $n = 5$, $p = 2$):

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 \\ 8 & 4 \\ 10 & 5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Verify the data is centered.**
Column means: $\bar{x}_1 = 6$, $\bar{x}_2 = 3$. Subtract them to get the centered matrix.

**Step 2 — Compute the covariance matrix.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{C} &= \dfrac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 4 & 2 \\ 6 & 3 \\ 8 & 4 \\ 10 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 220 & 110 \\ 110 & 55 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 44 & 22 \\ 22 & 11 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the eigenvalue equation $\det(\mathbf{C} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\det \begin{bmatrix} 44 - \lambda & 22 \\ 22 & 11 - \lambda \end{bmatrix} &= 0 \\
(44 - \lambda)(11 - \lambda) - 22 \cdot 22 &= 0 \\
484 - 44\lambda - 11\lambda + \lambda^2 - 484 &= 0 \\
\lambda^2 - 55\lambda &= 0 \\
\lambda(\lambda - 55) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\lambda_1 = 55$ and $\lambda_2 = 0$.

**Step 4 — Find the first eigenvector** (for $\lambda_1 = 55$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\mathbf{C} - 55 \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{w}_1 &= 0 \\
\begin{bmatrix} -11 & 22 \\ 22 & -44 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} w_{11} \\ w_{12} \end{bmatrix} &= 0 \\
-11 w_{11} + 22 w_{12} &= 0 \\
\Rightarrow w_{11} &= 2 w_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalize: $\lVert \mathbf{w}_1 \rVert = \sqrt{w_{11}^2 + w_{12}^2} = \sqrt{4 w_{12}^2 + w_{12}^2} = \sqrt{5} \, \vert w_{12} \vert$.

Setting $w_{12} = 1/\sqrt{5}$:

$$
\mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2/\sqrt{5} \\ 1/\sqrt{5} \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.894 \\ 0.447 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Explained Variance Ratio.**

$$
\text{EVR}_1 = \dfrac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2} = \dfrac{55}{55 + 0} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The data is **perfectly collinear** (lying on a single line $x_2 = 0.5 x_1$). One principal component captures 100% of the variance. The data can be reduced from 2D to 1D without any information loss. The reduced representation is $\mathbf{z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}_1$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Normalization Techniques

| Property | Min-Max | Z-Score | Decimal Scaling |
|---|---|---|---|
| Range | Bounded $[0,1]$ | Unbounded | Bounded $[-1,1]$ |
| Sensitivity to outliers | **High** | Low | Moderate |
| Use case | Neural networks, image processing | When $\mu, \sigma$ meaningful | Quick, simple rescaling |
| Requires knowing | $\min, \max$ | $\mu, \sigma$ | Only $j$ (number of digits) |
| Preserves distribution shape | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Preserves zero entries | No | Yes | Yes |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

| Property | PCA | LDA | t-SNE | Feature Selection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Unsupervised | Supervised | Unsupervised | Either |
| Linearity | Linear | Linear | Non-linear | N/A |
| Max dimensions | $p$ | $C-1$ | 2 or 3 | $p$ |
| Preserves | Global variance | Class separability | Local neighborhoods | Original features |
| Interpretability | Moderate (linear combos) | Moderate | Low | **High** (original features) |
| Computational cost | $O(p^3)$ for eigendecomp. | $O(p^3)$ | $O(n^2)$ | Varies |
| Sensitive to scaling | Yes (needs normalization) | Yes | No | No |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Model answers are deliberately concise but complete. Examiners typically allocate 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the formula/method, and 1 mark for an example or justification.

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between feature selection and feature extraction. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Feature Selection | Feature Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Selects a subset of original features | Creates new features by transforming original ones |
| Output | A subset $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \{1, \dots, p\}$ | A new feature space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ |
| Interpretability | High (original features retained) | Lower (often linear combinations) |
| Example | Removing `age` from a model after correlation analysis | PCA projecting 100D data to 5D |

**Example of feature selection:** Using the **Information Gain** criterion to drop low-scoring attributes. **Example of feature extraction:** Applying **PCA** to project a 1000-dimensional gene expression dataset onto its first 10 principal components.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is z-score normalization? State the formula. Why is it preferred over min-max normalization for outlier-prone data?**

**Model Answer:**

Z-score normalization transforms a value $x$ into a standardized score $z$ with mean 0 and standard deviation 1:

$$z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

It is preferred over min-max normalization for outlier-prone data because min-max scales strictly to $[\min, \max]$, so a single extreme outlier squashes all remaining values into a tiny range. Z-score uses the mean and standard deviation, which are less sensitive to extreme values, producing a more robust scaling. Outliers are easily detected using the convention $\vert z \vert > 3$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on Module 1 carry 14 marks each and offer an internal choice. Two independent alternative questions are provided below.

---

### Question B-A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the major data preprocessing tasks in data mining with a neat block diagram. Discuss in detail any **two techniques** for handling missing values in a dataset. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is dimensionality reduction? Explain **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** algorithm in detail with the necessary mathematical formulation. Apply PCA to find the first principal component for the following 2D dataset (centered): **(7 marks)**

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & -1 \\ -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The four major preprocessing tasks are:

1. **Data Cleaning** — handle missing values, noise, outliers, inconsistencies.
2. **Data Integration** — merge multiple sources, resolve schema conflicts and redundancy.
3. **Data Transformation** — normalize, smooth, aggregate, discretize.
4. **Data Reduction** — reduce dimensionality, numerosity, or compress data.

(Refer to **Diagram 1** in Section 4 for the block diagram — 2 marks)

**Two techniques for handling missing values:**

**Technique 1 — Mean / Median / Mode Imputation (3 marks)**

For numerical attributes, missing entries are replaced by:
- **Mean** $\mu$ — if the data is approximately symmetric.
- **Median** — if the data is skewed (more robust to outliers).

For categorical attributes, the **mode** (most frequent value) is used. The advantage is simplicity and preservation of sample size. The disadvantage is that it reduces variance and ignores correlations between attributes.

**Technique 2 — Regression-Based Imputation (2 marks)**

A regression model is built using the attribute with missing values as the target and the other attributes as predictors. The predicted value from the fitted regression replaces the missing entry. This method preserves inter-attribute correlations and is more accurate than simple mean imputation, though it underestimates variance.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Dimensionality reduction is the process of reducing the number of random variables (features) under consideration by obtaining a set of principal variables that retain most of the original information.

**PCA Algorithm (3 marks):**

1. Center the data by subtracting the mean from each feature.
2. Compute the covariance matrix $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$.
3. Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $\mathbf{C}$.
4. Sort eigenpairs in descending order of eigenvalue.
5. Select the top $k$ eigenvectors to form the projection matrix $\mathbf{W}_k$.
6. Transform the data: $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}_k$.

**Mathematical formulation (1 mark):** The optimal direction $\mathbf{w}$ satisfies $\mathbf{C} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$, derived via Lagrangian optimization of $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1$.

**Numerical application to the given data (2 marks):**

The data is already centered (column sums = 0). Compute:

$$
\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 15 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{C} = \dfrac{1}{5} \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 10 \\ 10 & 15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Eigenvalues: $\lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 \approx 4.5616, \lambda_2 \approx 0.4384$.

Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(2 - 4.5616) w_{11} + 2 w_{12} &= 0 \\
-2.5616 \, w_{11} + 2 w_{12} &= 0 \\
w_{12} &= 1.2808 \, w_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalizing: $\lVert \mathbf{w}_1 \rVert = \sqrt{w_{11}^2 + 1.6404 \, w_{11}^2} = \sqrt{2.6404} \, \vert w_{11} \vert \approx 1.625 \, \vert w_{11} \vert$.

So $\mathbf{w}_1 \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.615 \\ 0.788 \end{bmatrix}$.

**EVR**$_1 = \dfrac{4.5616}{5.0000} \approx 91.2\%$, indicating the first PC alone captures most of the variance. (2 marks)

---

### Question B-B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With suitable examples, explain the following normalization techniques: **(i)** Min-max normalization, **(ii)** Z-score normalization, and **(iii)** Decimal scaling normalization. Also state one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Curse of Dimensionality**? Explain how PCA mitigates this curse. Compare and contrast PCA with **LDA** in a tabular form covering at least 5 parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Min-Max Normalization (2 marks):**

$$
x' = \dfrac{x - \min(x)}{\max(x) - \min(x)}
$$

*Example:* For $x = 35$ in the range $[20, 80]$: $x' = (35 - 20)/(80 - 20) = 15/60 = 0.25$.

*Limitation:* Highly sensitive to outliers, as the denominator $\max - \min$ is dominated by extreme values, squashing the rest of the data into a narrow range.

**(ii) Z-Score Normalization (2 marks):**

$$
z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

*Example:* If $\mu = 50, \sigma = 10$ and $x = 70$, then $z = (70 - 50)/10 = 2.0$.

*Limitation:* Assumes the data is approximately normal; for heavily skewed distributions, the standardized values can be misleading. Also, the resulting range is unbounded.

**(iii) Decimal Scaling Normalization (1 mark):**

$$
x' = \dfrac{x}{10^{j}}
$$

where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max \vert x' \vert < 1$.

*Example:* For values in the range $[-986, 789]$, $j = 3$, so divide all by 1000.

*Limitation:* Not intuitive to interpret; $j$ depends on the largest absolute value, which can change as new data arrives.

**Comparison summary (2 marks):** Z-score is best when distribution is Gaussian; min-max is best for neural networks and image pixels; decimal scaling is best for quick normalization when magnitudes span powers of 10.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Curse of Dimensionality (2 marks):** As the number of features $p$ grows, the feature space becomes increasingly sparse, distance metrics lose discriminative power, computational cost explodes, and models overfit to noise. This was formalized by Bellman (1961).

**How PCA mitigates the curse (2 marks):** PCA projects the original $p$-dimensional data onto $k \ll p$ principal components that capture the directions of maximum variance. This:
- Removes redundant and noisy features.
- Reduces overfitting.
- Decreases computational cost (downstream algorithms scale better in low dimensions).
- Mitigates sparsity by compressing the data into a dense subspace.

**Comparison Table — PCA vs LDA (3 marks):**

| Parameter | PCA | LDA |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Unsupervised | Supervised (needs labels) |
| Objective | Maximize total variance | Maximize class separability |
| Max output dimensions | $p$ | $C - 1$ |
| Uses class labels | No | Yes |
| Sensitivity to feature scaling | High (needs normalization) | High (needs normalization) |
| Interpretability | Linear combinations of features | Discriminant directions between classes |
| Computational complexity | $O(p^3)$ | $O(p^3)$, but lower effective dim |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic (as observed in recent KTU valuation cycles):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to center the data before PCA.** Marks deducted at the very first step of any PCA numerical.
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ with $\mathbf{X} \mathbf{X}^{\top}$.** The covariance matrix is $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ for data with shape $n \times p$.
> 3. **Mixing up $n$ and $n - 1$ in the covariance denominator.** The KTU convention (matching NumPy and most textbooks) is $\frac{1}{n}$ for PCA.
> 4. **Not normalizing eigenvectors** to unit length when reporting them.
> 5. **Forgetting the orthogonality constraint** between successive principal components.
> 6. **Confusing PCA (unsupervised) with LDA (supervised)** when class labels are available.
> 7. **Stating definitions but not writing formulas** in Part A — at least one formula earns the second mark.
> 8. **Providing only the formula for min-max without the derivation logic** when explicitly asked to "explain".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this just before entering the examination hall.**

### Data Preprocessing — Essentials

- Four major tasks: **Cleaning, Integration, Transformation, Reduction**.
- Missing value strategies: ignore, mean/median/mode, regression, KNN, indicator variable.
- Noise handling: **binning, regression, clustering, outlier detection**.
- Min-max scales to $[0,1]$; bounded, sensitive to outliers.
- Z-score gives mean 0, variance 1; unbounded, more robust.
- Decimal scaling divides by $10^j$.
- Discretization: binning, histogram, clustering, decision-tree based.
- Data integration challenges: entity ID, redundancy, duplicates, value conflicts.

### Dimensionality Reduction — Essentials

- **Feature Selection**: Filter (chi-square, IG), Wrapper (forward/backward), Embedded (Lasso, trees).
- **Feature Extraction**: PCA, LDA, t-SNE, SVD, autoencoders.
- PCA seeks direction $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes projected variance $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{C} \mathbf{w}$.
- The optimum is the **eigenvector corresponding to the largest eigenvalue** of the covariance matrix.
- The covariance matrix is $\mathbf{C} = \frac{1}{n} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ (after centering).
- Successive PCs are orthogonal eigenvectors sorted by descending eigenvalue.
- Choose $k$ such that **Cumulative Explained Variance Ratio** $\geq 95\%$.
- Reconstruction error when keeping $k$ PCs: $J = \sum_{i=k+1}^{p} \lambda_i$.
- PCA is **unsupervised** and **linear**; LDA is **supervised** and **linear**; t-SNE is **unsupervised** and **non-linear**.
- PCA is **sensitive to feature scaling** — always normalize beforehand.
- Curse of dimensionality: sparsity, distance collapse, overfitting, computational blowup.

### Quick Mnemonics for the Exam

- **"C-C-E-S-S-P"** for PCA steps: **C**enter → (optional) **C**ompute covariance → **E**igendecompose → **S**ort → **S**elect top-k → **P**roject.
- **"MMD"** for normalization: **M**in-max, **M**ean (z-score), **D**ecimal scaling.
- **"CIDR"** for preprocessing tasks: **C**lean, **I**ntegrate, **D**iscretize/transform, **R**educe.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Descriptive Statistics - Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT611 - DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Descriptive Statistics - Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Descriptive Statistics** is the branch of statistics that involves the **organization, summarization, and presentation of data** in a meaningful and informative way, without making inferences beyond the observed dataset. It converts raw, unprocessed observations into interpretable numerical summaries.

Within Descriptive Statistics, two principal families of summary measures are studied:

1. **Measures of Central Tendency (Location):** A single value that attempts to **describe a dataset by identifying its central position** — it represents a typical or average value. The three classical measures are the **Mean, Median, and Mode**.

2. **Measures of Dispersion (Variability/Scatter):** These describe **how stretched or squeezed a given distribution is**. They quantify the **degree to which data points deviate from the central value**, indicating whether observations cluster tightly around the mean or are widely scattered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The student must master (a) the computation of Mean, Median, Mode for both *ungrouped* and *grouped* data, and (b) the computation and interpretation of Range, Quartile Deviation, Mean Deviation, Variance, and Standard Deviation. Skewness, Kurtosis, and Coefficient of Variation are also examinable extensions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine **50 KTU students lining up for the canteen** based on their monthly pocket-money (in ₹).

- **Central Tendency** asks: *"What is the typical pocket-money of a KTU student?"* — One number that "stands in the middle" for the whole crowd.
- **Dispersion** asks: *"Are all students getting roughly the same amount, or is one getting ₹500 while another gets ₹5,000?"* — A measure of **how spread out** the line is.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Target Shooter:**
> Imagine a shooter firing 10 shots at a target.
> - The **Mean** is where the *bullseye* is aimed (center).
> - The **Dispersion** is the *scatter* of bullet holes around that center.
> - **Low dispersion + Correct mean = Expert shooter.** **High dispersion = Inconsistent shooter**, no matter where the mean lies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both are needed:** Two datasets can have the **same mean (₹2,000)** but **vastly different dispersions**. For example, [1000, 2000, 3000] vs [0, 2000, 4000] — same mean, different realities. Hence, central tendency alone is *misleading* without dispersion.

---

### 1.3 Key Nomenclature

- **N** → Size of the population (or total number of observations).
- **n** → Size of the sample.
- **x_i** → The i-th observation in the dataset.
- **Σx** → Greek capital *sigma* — denotes **summation** (sum of all x values).
- **f_i** → Frequency of the i-th class (in grouped data).
- **x̄** → Sample mean (pronounced *x-bar*).
- **μ** → Population mean (Greek letter *mu*).
- **σ²** → Population variance; **s²** → Sample variance.
- **σ** → Population standard deviation; **s** → Sample standard deviation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always distinguish between *sample* and *population* formulas. Board questions often specify "for the data given" (sample) versus "from the following frequency distribution" (could be either). Use **x̄, s, n** for samples and **μ, σ², N** for populations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Measures of Central Tendency — Logical Breakdown

#### A. Arithmetic Mean (AM)

- **Logic:** The AM is the *balancing point* of the data. If you placed all observations on a perfectly flat seesaw, the AM is the exact fulcrum position.
- **Property 1 (Algebraic):** The sum of deviations from the mean is *always zero* → **Σ(x_i − x̄) = 0**.
- **Property 2 (Sensitivity):** AM is highly sensitive to *extreme values* (outliers) — this is its main weakness.
- **Step-Deviation Method:** Used for grouped data with large class widths to simplify computation.

#### B. Median (M or Md)

- **Logic:** The Median is the *positional middle* — the value that splits the dataset into two equal halves. **50% of data lies below the median; 50% above.**
- **Robustness:** Median is *not affected by extreme values*, making it the preferred measure of central tendency for **skewed distributions** (e.g., income, house prices).
- **For odd n:** Median is the **((n+1)/2)-th** value after sorting.
- **For even n:** Median is the **average of (n/2)-th and ((n/2)+1)-th** values.

#### C. Mode (Z or Mo)

- **Logic:** The Mode is the *most frequently occurring* value in the dataset. It is the only measure of central tendency applicable to **nominal (qualitative) data** (e.g., eye colour, blood group).
- **Property:** A dataset can be **unimodal, bimodal, or multimodal**. A distribution with no repeating value has *no mode*.
- **Grouped Data:** Mode lies in the **modal class** (the class with highest frequency).

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Relationship (used by examiners for quick checks):**
> **Mode ≈ 3 × Median − 2 × Mean**
> For *moderately skewed* distributions, this approximation holds. KTU questions sometimes verify this relationship experimentally.

---

### 2.2 Measures of Dispersion — Logical Breakdown

#### A. Range (R)

- **Logic:** Simplest measure — the *total spread* between the highest and lowest values.
- **Drawback:** Uses only *two extreme points*; ignores the distribution of the rest of the data. **Highly sensitive to outliers.**

#### B. Quartile Deviation (QD) / Semi-Interquartile Range

- **Logic:** Based on **Q₁ (25th percentile) and Q₃ (75th percentile)**. Measures the spread of the **middle 50%** of the data — robust to outliers.
- **Interquartile Range (IQR):** **IQR = Q₃ − Q₁**.

#### C. Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) / Mean Deviation (MD)

- **Logic:** The *average of absolute deviations* from a central value (mean, median, or mode). It tells us "on average, how far does a typical data point lie from the center?"

#### D. Variance (σ² / s²) and Standard Deviation (σ / s)

- **Logic:** The most powerful and widely used dispersion measure. It squares each deviation from the mean (squared deviations are always non-negative, avoiding cancellation), averages them, and takes the square root for SD.
- **Why squaring?** It penalizes *large deviations more heavily* than small ones — making SD sensitive to *outliers* and *spread of the tails*.
- **Degrees of Freedom (Why n−1 in sample SD):** When computing sample variance, we lose 1 degree of freedom because we must first estimate the sample mean (x̄) from the same data. Hence we divide by **(n − 1)** — this is called **Bessel's Correction** and produces an *unbiased* estimator of population variance.

#### E. Coefficient of Variation (CV)

- **Logic:** A *relative* (dimensionless) measure of dispersion. It expresses the standard deviation as a **percentage of the mean**, allowing comparison of variability across datasets with **different units or vastly different means** (e.g., comparing the variability of heights in cm vs. weights in kg).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

#### Table 1 — Measures of Central Tendency

| **Measure** | **Ungrouped Data Formula** | **Grouped Data Formula** |
|-------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Arithmetic Mean** | $x̄ = \dfrac{\Sigma x_i}{n}$ | $x̄ = \dfrac{\Sigma f_i x_i}{\Sigma f_i}$ |
| **Median (Odd n)** | Value at position $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$ | $M = L + \left(\dfrac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$ |
| **Median (Even n)** | Mean of $\dfrac{n}{2}$-th and $\left(\dfrac{n}{2}+1\right)$-th values | $M = L + \left(\dfrac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$ |
| **Mode** | Most frequent value | $Z = L + \left(\dfrac{f_1 - f_0}{2f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) \cdot h$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend for Grouped Data Formulas:**
> $L$ = lower class boundary of median/modal class, $N = \Sigma f_i$, $F$ = cumulative frequency *before* median class, $f$ = frequency of median class, $h$ = class width.
> $f_1$ = freq. of modal class, $f_0$ = freq. of class *preceding* modal class, $f_2$ = freq. of class *succeeding* modal class.

#### Table 2 — Measures of Dispersion

| **Measure** | **Sample Formula (n divisor)** | **Population Formula (N divisor)** |
|-------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Range** | $R = x_{max} - x_{min}$ | Same as sample |
| **Quartile Deviation** | $QD = \dfrac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$ | Same as sample |
| **Mean Deviation (about mean)** | $MD = \dfrac{\Sigma \vert x_i - x̄ \vert}{n}$ | $MD = \dfrac{\Sigma \vert x_i - \mu \vert}{N}$ |
| **Variance** | $s^2 = \dfrac{\Sigma (x_i - x̄)^2}{n-1}$ | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\Sigma (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}$ |
| **Standard Deviation** | $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma (x_i - x̄)^2}{n-1}}$ | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma (x_i - \mu)^2}{N}}$ |
| **Coefficient of Variation** | $CV = \dfrac{s}{x̄} \times 100 \,\%$ | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100 \,\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shortcut Variance Identity (KTU Favourite):**
> $$s^2 = \dfrac{\Sigma x_i^2}{n-1} - \dfrac{(\Sigma x_i)^2}{n(n-1)}$$
> or equivalently:
> $$\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\Sigma x_i^2}{N} - \mu^2$$
> Use this to save time when individual deviations are large or unwieldy.

#### Table 3 — Properties at a Glance

| **Property** | **Mean** | **Median** | **Mode** |
|--------------|----------|------------|----------|
| Affected by outliers? | **Yes (heavily)** | No | Slightly |
| Unique value? | Always unique | Always unique | Not always |
| Applicable to qualitative data? | No | No | **Yes** |
| Algebraic properties (sum of deviations = 0)? | **Yes** | No | No |
| Best for skewed data? | No | **Yes** | Sometimes |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Quality Control:** A factory producing bolts with *target diameter* = 10 mm and *tolerance* ± 0.05 mm uses **standard deviation** as the *Process Capability Index* (Cpk). Low SD = consistent product.
- **Civil/Structural Engineering:** SD of concrete compressive strength determines **safety factors** in building design.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Mean and SD of signal noise help compute the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.
- **Data Science / ML:** Variance and SD are foundational for **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)**, **Z-score normalization**, and **outlier detection** (e.g., points beyond μ ± 3σ are flagged as anomalies in a Gaussian distribution).
- **Economics & Finance:** **CV** is used to compare *risk per unit of return* across different investment portfolios.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Variance Uses Squared Deviations (and not Absolute Deviations)

**Problem:** Why do we square deviations from the mean instead of just taking absolute values (as in Mean Deviation)?

**Step 1:** We want a measure of "typical distance" of data from the center.
A natural candidate is:

$$D = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert$$

**Step 2:** This is mathematically valid (and is the *Mean Deviation*), but it is **not differentiable at the point x_i = x̄** because the absolute value function has a sharp corner there. This makes it mathematically inconvenient for further analysis (e.g., optimization, regression, ML loss functions).

**Step 3:** To get a *smooth, differentiable* function, we square the deviations:

$$D' = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**Step 4:** The derivative of (x_i − x̄)² with respect to x̄ is **−2(x_i − x̄)**, which is well-defined and continuous. Setting this derivative to zero to *minimize* D' gives:

$$\frac{d}{d\bar{x}} \left[ \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \right] = -2 \sum (x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$$

This confirms that **x̄ = (Σx_i)/n** is the value that minimizes the sum of squared deviations. The mean is therefore the *least-squares estimator* of central tendency.

**Step 5:** Taking the square root of the variance gives us a measure in the **same units as the original data** (the standard deviation), making it interpretable. **Hence: SD = √Variance.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Bias-Corrected Sample Variance (Why n − 1?)

We want the sample variance **s²** to be an *unbiased estimator* of the population variance **σ²**, i.e., **E[s²] = σ²**.

**Step 1:** Suppose we have a population with true mean μ and true variance σ². We draw a sample of size n and compute:

$$s^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squared term and use the identity that:

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2$$

**Step 3:** Take the expected value. After some algebra (using E[Σx_i²] = n(σ² + μ²) and E[Σx_i]² = n(n−1)σ² + n²μ²), we get:

$$E\left[\frac{1}{n} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2\right] = \frac{n-1}{n} \sigma^2$$

**Step 4:** To make the estimator *unbiased*, we multiply by **n/(n−1)**:

$$E\left[\frac{1}{n-1} \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2\right] = \sigma^2$$

**Conclusion:** Dividing by **(n − 1)** removes the systematic downward bias, producing an unbiased estimator. Hence, the sample variance uses **n − 1** as the denominator (Bessel's Correction).

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Ungrouped Data (Full Computation)

**Problem:** Compute Mean, Median, Mode, Range, QD, MD, Variance, SD, and CV for:
**Data:** 12, 15, 18, 22, 22, 25, 30, 36

**Given:** n = 8 (already sorted in ascending order).

#### Step A: Mean

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\Sigma x_i}{n} = \frac{12+15+18+22+22+25+30+36}{8} = \frac{180}{8} = 22.5$$

#### Step B: Median

n = 8 (even), so Median = mean of 4th and 5th values.

$$M = \frac{x_{(4)} + x_{(5)}}{2} = \frac{22 + 22}{2} = 22$$

#### Step C: Mode

The value 22 appears twice; all others appear once.

$$Z = 22$$

#### Step D: Range

$$R = x_{max} - x_{min} = 36 - 12 = 24$$

#### Step E: Quartile Deviation

Position of Q₁ = (n+1)/4 = 9/4 = 2.25 → between 2nd and 3rd value.
$$Q_1 = x_{(2)} + 0.25 \cdot (x_{(3)} - x_{(2)}) = 15 + 0.25 \cdot (18 - 15) = 15 + 0.75 = 15.75$$

Position of Q₃ = 3(n+1)/4 = 6.75 → between 6th and 7th value.
$$Q_3 = x_{(6)} + 0.75 \cdot (x_{(7)} - x_{(6)}) = 25 + 0.75 \cdot (30 - 25) = 25 + 3.75 = 28.75$$

$$QD = \frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2} = \frac{28.75 - 15.75}{2} = \frac{13}{2} = 6.5$$

#### Step F: Mean Deviation about the Mean

| $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 22.5 \vert$ |
|:-----:|:----------------------:|
| 12    | 10.5                   |
| 15    | 7.5                    |
| 18    | 4.5                    |
| 22    | 0.5                    |
| 22    | 0.5                    |
| 25    | 2.5                    |
| 30    | 7.5                    |
| 36    | 13.5                   |
| **Σ** | **47.0**               |

$$MD_{\bar{x}} = \frac{47.0}{8} = 5.875$$

#### Step G: Variance and Standard Deviation

| $x_i$ | $(x_i - 22.5)^2$ |
|:-----:|:----------------:|
| 12    | 110.25           |
| 15    | 56.25            |
| 18    | 20.25            |
| 22    | 0.25             |
| 22    | 0.25             |
| 25    | 6.25             |
| 30    | 56.25            |
| 36    | 182.25           |
| **Σ** | **432.00**       |

Sample variance:
$$s^2 = \frac{432}{n-1} = \frac{432}{7} \approx 61.714$$

$$s = \sqrt{61.714} \approx 7.856$$

#### Step H: Coefficient of Variation

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{7.856}{22.5} \times 100\% \approx 34.92\%$$

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Mean = 22.5, Median = 22, Mode = 22
- Range = 24, QD = 6.5, MD = 5.875
- s² = 61.714, s = 7.856, CV = 34.92%

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Check:** Mode (22) ≈ 3 × Median (22) − 2 × Mean (22.5) = 66 − 45 = 21. Close to 22 ✓ (small skew).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Grouped Data (Median and Mode Formulas)

**Problem:** Find the Median and Mode for the following frequency distribution.

| Class Interval | Frequency ($f_i$) | Cumulative Frequency ($cf$) |
|:--------------:|:----------------:|:--------------------------:|
| 0 – 10         | 5                | 5                          |
| 10 – 20        | 12               | 17                         |
| 20 – 30        | 18               | 35                         |
| 30 – 40        | 10               | 45                         |
| 40 – 50        | 5                | 50                         |

N = 50, h = 10.

#### Median Calculation:

**Step 1:** Find N/2 = 50/2 = 25.

**Step 2:** Identify the *Median Class* — the class whose cumulative frequency first exceeds 25. The cf just exceeding 25 is 35, which lies in the class **20 – 30**.

So: L = 20, f = 18, F = cf of class *preceding* median class = 17, h = 10.

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$$

$$M = 20 + \left(\frac{25 - 17}{18}\right) \cdot 10$$

$$M = 20 + \left(\frac{8}{18}\right) \cdot 10$$

$$M = 20 + 4.444 \approx 24.44$$

#### Mode Calculation:

**Step 1:** Identify the *Modal Class* — the class with the **highest frequency**. Here, the class **20 – 30** has f = 18 (highest).

So: L = 20, h = 10, f₁ = 18, f₀ = 12 (preceding class), f₂ = 10 (succeeding class).

**Step 2:** Apply the mode formula:

$$Z = L + \left(\frac{f_1 - f_0}{2f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) \cdot h$$

$$Z = 20 + \left(\frac{18 - 12}{2(18) - 12 - 10}\right) \cdot 10$$

$$Z = 20 + \left(\frac{6}{36 - 22}\right) \cdot 10$$

$$Z = 20 + \left(\frac{6}{14}\right) \cdot 10$$

$$Z = 20 + 4.286 \approx 24.29$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Median ≈ 24.44, Mode ≈ 24.29. The closeness of Median and Mode (with Mean slightly higher) indicates *mild positive skew* in the distribution.

---

### 3.5 Step-Deviation Method for Grouped Mean (Large Class Widths)

**When to use:** When class widths are large and class marks are spread out, computing Σf_i·x_i directly becomes cumbersome.

**Procedure:**
1. Choose an **assumed mean (A)** — typically the class mark of a middle or high-frequency class.
2. Compute deviations: $d_i = x_i - A$ (or $d_i = \dfrac{x_i - A}{h}$ for further simplification).
3. Apply: $\bar{x} = A + \dfrac{\Sigma f_i d_i}{\Sigma f_i}$ (or with h-multiplied back).

**Advantage:** Reduces arithmetic size of numbers — exam-friendly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Descriptive Statistical Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Descriptive Statistics Summary Measures"]
    rootB["Descriptive Statistics Summary Measures"]
    rootA --> ct["Measures of Central Tendency"]
    rootB --> disp["Measures of Dispersion"]
    ct --> m1["Arithmetic Mean"]
    ct --> m2["Median"]
    ct --> m3["Mode"]
    m1 --> m1a["Ungrouped: Sum / n"]
    m1 --> m1b["Grouped: Sum f x / Sum f"]
    m2 --> m2a["Odd n: Middle Value"]
    m2 --> m2b["Even n: Mean of Two Middles"]
    m2 --> m2c["Grouped: L + h bracket N/2-F over f"]
    m3 --> m3a["Ungrouped: Most Frequent Value"]
    m3 --> m3b["Grouped: L + h bracket f1-f0 over 2f1-f0-f2"]
    disp --> d1["Absolute Measures"]
    disp --> d2["Relative Measures"]
    d1 --> d1a["Range"]
    d1 --> d1b["Quartile Deviation QD"]
    d1 --> d1c["Mean Deviation MD"]
    d1 --> d1d["Variance and Standard Deviation"]
    d2 --> d2a["Coefficient of Range"]
    d2 --> d2b["Coefficient of QD"]
    d2 --> d2c["Coefficient of MD"]
    d2 --> d2d["Coefficient of Variation CV"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing the Right Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["Start: What is the Nature of the Data"]
    s1 --> s2["Is the Data Qualitative Nominal"]
    s2 -- Yes --> s3["Use MODE only option"]
    s2 -- No --> s4["Is the Data Highly Skewed or Has Outliers"]
    s4 -- Yes --> s5["Use MEDIAN as Central Tendency and IQR or QD as Dispersion"]
    s4 -- No --> s6["Use MEAN as Central Tendency and SD or Variance as Dispersion"]
    s5 --> s7["Are Units Different Across Datasets"]
    s6 --> s7
    s7 -- Yes --> s8["Use Coefficient of Variation CV for Comparison"]
    s7 -- No --> s9["Use Absolute Measures SD Variance directly"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Statistical Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw["Raw Dataset X1 X2 ... Xn"]
    raw --> pre["Preprocessing Sort Handle Outliers"]
    pre --> ct["Compute Central Tendency Mean Median Mode"]
    pre --> disp["Compute Dispersion Range QD MD Variance SD"]
    ct --> desc["Descriptive Summary Table"]
    disp --> desc
    desc --> inf["Used for Inferential Statistics Confidence Intervals Hypothesis Tests"]
    desc --> ml["Used in Machine Learning Feature Scaling Outlier Detection PCA"]
    inf --> decision["Engineering or Scientific Decision"]
    ml --> decision
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice how **Central Tendency and Dispersion form parallel preprocessing pipelines** that converge into a *single descriptive summary*. This summary then becomes the **input for higher-level tasks** — statistical inference (KTU Module 3/4) and machine learning (data science applications).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each — Short Answer / Definition Type)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following with one example each:**
(a) Quartile Deviation
(b) Coefficient of Variation

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Quartile Deviation (QD):**
Quartile Deviation, also known as the *Semi-Interquartile Range*, is a measure of dispersion based on the **interquartile range (IQR)**. It is defined as **half of the difference between the third quartile (Q₃) and the first quartile (Q₁)**:

$$QD = \frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}$$

**Example:** For data {4, 6, 8, 10, 12}, Q₁ = 6, Q₃ = 10, so QD = (10 − 6)/2 = **2**.

**(b) Coefficient of Variation (CV):**
The Coefficient of Variation is a *relative* (dimensionless and unit-free) measure of dispersion, expressed as the **ratio of the standard deviation to the mean, multiplied by 100%**:

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\%$$

**Example:** If a dataset has mean = 50 and SD = 10, then CV = (10/50) × 100 = **20%**. A lower CV indicates *less relative variability*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern (3 marks):** Expect 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for a clear definition in words, and 1 mark for a small numerical or contextual example.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between absolute and relative measures of dispersion. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Absolute Measures | Relative Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Expressed in the **same units as the original data**. | **Dimensionless / unit-free** (often expressed as %). |
| **Purpose** | Quantify the *absolute scatter* of a single dataset. | Compare variability **across datasets** with different units or scales. |
| **Examples** | Range, Quartile Deviation, Mean Deviation, Standard Deviation, Variance. | Coefficient of Range, Coefficient of QD, Coefficient of MD, **Coefficient of Variation (CV)**. |

**Examples with values:**
- *Absolute:* SD of heights in a class = 5 cm.
- *Relative:* CV of heights = (5/165) × 100 = 3.03%; CV of weights = (4/60) × 100 = 6.67% → weights have higher *relative* variability than heights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Relative Measures Exist:** Heights (in cm) and weights (in kg) cannot be compared using raw SDs because units differ. CV normalizes both to a percentage scale, enabling direct comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers a *choice* (OR option). You must attempt **one** of the two. Each question typically has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage: Ungrouped Data

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

The marks (out of 50) obtained by 10 students in a Data Analysis class are:

**42, 35, 50, 28, 45, 38, 50, 40, 32, 48**

**(a) Calculate the mean, median, and mode of the data. Comment on the shape of the distribution.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the range, quartile deviation, variance, standard deviation, and coefficient of variation. Interpret the CV.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Arrange data in ascending order:**

28, 32, 35, 38, 40, 42, 45, 48, 50, 50

n = 10 (even)

**Step 2 — Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{28+32+35+38+40+42+45+48+50+50}{10} = \frac{408}{10} = 40.8$$

**Step 3 — Median (n = 10, even):**

$$M = \frac{x_{(5)} + x_{(6)}}{2} = \frac{40 + 42}{2} = 41$$

**Step 4 — Mode:**

The value 50 occurs twice; all others occur once. Hence:

$$Z = 50$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Shape:**

- Mean (40.8) < Median (41) < Mode (50).
- The fact that **Mode is much larger than both Mean and Median** indicates a **negative (left) skew** in the distribution.
- However, Mean ≈ Median, suggesting the bulk of data is fairly symmetric; the high Mode (50) occurs because the maximum score was achieved by 2 students, slightly inflating the mode.

**Conclusion:** The distribution is **approximately symmetric with a very slight left skew**. The high Mode is influenced by the duplicate maximum score.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Range:**

$$R = x_{max} - x_{min} = 50 - 28 = 22$$

**Step 2 — Quartile Deviation:**

Using position formula (n+1)/4 and 3(n+1)/4:

- Q₁ position = 11/4 = 2.75 → Q₁ = x₂ + 0.75(x₃ − x₂) = 32 + 0.75(35 − 32) = 32 + 2.25 = **34.25**
- Q₃ position = 33/4 = 8.25 → Q₃ = x₈ + 0.25(x₉ − x₈) = 48 + 0.25(50 − 48) = 48 + 0.5 = **48.5**

$$QD = \frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2} = \frac{48.5 - 34.25}{2} = \frac{14.25}{2} = 7.125$$

**Step 3 — Variance (using shortcut formula for efficiency):**

$$\Sigma x_i = 408, \quad \Sigma x_i^2 = 28^2 + 32^2 + 35^2 + 38^2 + 40^2 + 42^2 + 45^2 + 48^2 + 50^2 + 50^2$$

Computing each:
- 28² = 784, 32² = 1024, 35² = 1225, 38² = 1444, 40² = 1600
- 42² = 1764, 45² = 2025, 48² = 2304, 50² = 2500, 50² = 2500

$$\Sigma x_i^2 = 784 + 1024 + 1225 + 1444 + 1600 + 1764 + 2025 + 2304 + 2500 + 2500 = 17170$$

$$s^2 = \frac{\Sigma x_i^2 - \frac{(\Sigma x_i)^2}{n}}{n-1} = \frac{17170 - \frac{(408)^2}{10}}{9}$$

$$s^2 = \frac{17170 - \frac{166464}{10}}{9} = \frac{17170 - 16646.4}{9} = \frac{523.6}{9} \approx 58.18$$

**Step 4 — Standard Deviation:**

$$s = \sqrt{58.18} \approx 7.628$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{7.628}{40.8} \times 100\% \approx 18.70\%$$

**Interpretation of CV:** A CV of **18.70%** indicates *moderate relative variability* in the dataset. Roughly, the standard deviation is about 18.7% of the mean, which means the marks show *reasonable consistency* around the average, but with notable spread. (For comparison, CV < 15% is usually considered *low variability*; CV > 30% is *high variability*.)

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Coverage: Grouped Data

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

The following table shows the daily wages (in ₹) of 60 workers in a factory:

| Daily Wages (₹)   | Number of Workers ($f_i$) |
|:-----------------:|:------------------------:|
| 100 – 200         | 8                        |
| 200 – 300         | 14                       |
| 300 – 400         | 18                       |
| 400 – 500         | 12                       |
| 500 – 600         | 6                        |
| 600 – 700         | 2                        |

**(a) Calculate the arithmetic mean using the step-deviation method. Find the median and the mode.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the standard deviation and the coefficient of variation. State which measure of central tendency is most appropriate for this data and why.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Construct the computation table:**

| Class | $f_i$ | Class Mark $x_i$ | $d_i = \dfrac{x_i - A}{h}$, with A = 350, h = 100 | $f_i d_i$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:----------------:|:---------------------------------------------:|:---------:|
| 100–200 | 8  | 150  | −2  | −16 |
| 200–300 | 14 | 250  | −1  | −14 |
| 300–400 | 18 | 350  | 0 (A) | 0 |
| 400–500 | 12 | 450  | 1   | 12 |
| 500–600 | 6  | 550  | 2   | 12 |
| 600–700 | 2  | 650  | 3   | 6 |
| **Total** | **60** | — | — | **0** |

(Choosing A = 350 in the class 300–400 because it is the middle class with high frequency.)

**Step 2 — Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = A + \frac{\Sigma f_i d_i}{\Sigma f_i} \cdot h = 350 + \frac{0}{60} \cdot 100 = 350$$

So, **Mean = ₹350**.

**Step 3 — Median:**

N/2 = 60/2 = 30. Cumulative frequencies: 8, 22, 40, 52, 58, 60. The cumulative frequency *first exceeding 30* is 40, lying in the class **300 – 400**.

So: L = 300, f = 18, F = 22, h = 100, N/2 = 30.

$$M = L + \left(\frac{\frac{N}{2} - F}{f}\right) \cdot h = 300 + \left(\frac{30 - 22}{18}\right) \cdot 100$$

$$M = 300 + \left(\frac{8}{18}\right) \cdot 100 = 300 + 44.44 \approx 344.44$$

So, **Median ≈ ₹344.44**.

**Step 4 — Mode:**

Modal class = class with highest frequency = **300 – 400** (f = 18).
So: L = 300, f₁ = 18, f₀ = 14, f₂ = 12, h = 100.

$$Z = L + \left(\frac{f_1 - f_0}{2f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) \cdot h = 300 + \left(\frac{18 - 14}{36 - 14 - 12}\right) \cdot 100$$

$$Z = 300 + \left(\frac{4}{10}\right) \cdot 100 = 300 + 40 = 340$$

So, **Mode = ₹340**.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Standard Deviation (using step-deviation extension):**

We additionally compute $f_i d_i^2$:

| $f_i$ | $d_i$ | $d_i^2$ | $f_i d_i^2$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| 8     | −2    | 4       | 32          |
| 14    | −1    | 1       | 14          |
| 18    | 0     | 0       | 0           |
| 12    | 1     | 1       | 12          |
| 6     | 2     | 4       | 24          |
| 2     | 3     | 9       | 18          |
| **Σ = 60** | — | — | **Σ = 100** |

**Step 2 — Standard Deviation formula (step-deviation method):**

$$\sigma = h \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Sigma f_i d_i^2}{\Sigma f_i} - \left(\frac{\Sigma f_i d_i}{\Sigma f_i}\right)^2}$$

Since $\Sigma f_i d_i = 0$ in our case (A was chosen as the actual mean):

$$\sigma = 100 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{100}{60} - 0^2} = 100 \cdot \sqrt{1.6667} = 100 \cdot 1.291 \approx 129.1$$

So, **Standard Deviation ≈ ₹129.1**.

> [!NOTE]
> If treating this as a *sample* (n − 1 divisor), the calculation would use a small correction. For grouped data representing the **entire factory population**, use the N divisor (as done above).

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{129.1}{350} \times 100\% \approx 36.89\%$$

**Step 4 — Most Appropriate Measure of Central Tendency:**

- The CV is **~36.89%**, which is **high**, indicating *substantial variability* in wages.
- Also, the **Mean (350) > Median (344.44) < Mode (340)** — wait, the relationship is **Mean > Median > Mode**.
- This order (Mean > Median > Mode) is the classical signature of a **positively (right) skewed distribution** — typical of *wage/salary data*, where a few high earners pull the mean to the right.
- **Conclusion:** For this positively skewed wage distribution, the **MEDIAN is the most appropriate measure of central tendency**, because it is **resistant to extreme values** (the high earners in the 500–700 bracket do not distort it). The mean is inflated by these high values.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Using n instead of (n−1) for sample variance** — A *very common* error. The question wording must be carefully read. "Compute the sample variance" → use **n − 1**. "Compute the population variance" → use **N**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the class boundary adjustment** in grouped median/mode — Students often use **class limits** (e.g., 100–200) instead of **class boundaries** (e.g., 99.5–200.5). For data with *exclusive* classes (continuous data), the limits and boundaries are identical. But for *inclusive* classes, you *must* add/subtract 0.5 (or the appropriate gap correction). Always check the question!
>
> 3. **Confusing the position of Q₁ and Q₃** — KTU uses the **(n+1)/4** convention. Some textbooks use **n/4**. Stick to KTU's convention: Q₁ position = (n+1)/4, Q₃ position = 3(n+1)/4.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in step-deviation method** — The deviations $d_i$ can be negative. Squaring them removes the sign, but the **Σf_i·d_i** in the mean formula must retain its sign. Mistake: students often square $d_i$ before multiplying by $f_i$ in the mean formula.
>
> 5. **Median class identification error** — The median class is identified by the *cumulative frequency first exceeding N/2*, **not** by the highest frequency (that is the **modal class**). These are often *different* classes!
>
> 6. **CV interpretation** — Many students compute CV correctly but fail to *interpret* it. Examiners expect a one-line qualitative statement (e.g., "low/moderate/high variability").
>
> 7. **Units of dispersion** — Variance has *squared units* (e.g., ₹²), while SD has *the same units* as data (e.g., ₹). Students often forget this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 — Descriptive Statistics Cheat Sheet:**

- **Central Tendency = "Where is the center?"** → Mean, Median, Mode.
- **Dispersion = "How spread out is the data?"** → Range, QD, MD, Variance, SD, CV.
- **Mean** is best for *symmetric* data; **Median** is best for *skewed* data; **Mode** is the only measure for *qualitative* data.
- **Standard Deviation** is the *most powerful* and *most commonly used* measure of dispersion.
- **Sample variance uses (n − 1) divisor** (Bessel's Correction); **Population variance uses N divisor**.
- **Shortcut variance formula:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\Sigma x^2}{N} - \mu^2$ (saves time in KTU exams).
- **CV** allows comparison of variability between datasets with *different units* (e.g., heights in cm vs. weights in kg).
- **Quartile Deviation (QD)** is *robust to outliers*; **SD** is *sensitive to outliers*.
- **Empirical relationship:** Mode ≈ 3 Median − 2 Mean (for moderately skewed data).
- **Step-deviation method** is preferred for grouped data with *large class marks* — reduces computational effort.
- **Median class** is identified by cf first exceeding N/2; **Modal class** is identified by *highest frequency*.
- **Grouped Median formula:** $M = L + \left(\dfrac{N/2 - F}{f}\right) \cdot h$
- **Grouped Mode formula:** $Z = L + \left(\dfrac{f_1 - f_0}{2f_1 - f_0 - f_2}\right) \cdot h$
- **Interpretation of CV:** < 15% → low variability; 15–30% → moderate; > 30% → high.
- **Units matter:** SD and Mean have the same units; Variance has squared units; CV is unitless (%).
- **Always read the question** to determine if it is a sample (use n − 1) or population (use N).
- **For KTU Module 2 specifically**, also briefly revise: **Skewness** (Pearson's coefficient = (Mean − Mode)/SD) and **Kurtosis** (peakedness of distribution) — these are *qualitative descriptors* of shape and often asked as 3-mark questions.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Descriptive Statistics: Measures of Central Tendency and Dispersion**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Association of Two Variables - Discrete Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT611 - DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Association of Two Variables - Discrete Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the framework of **Data Analysis**, **Association** refers to the statistical dependence or relationship between two categorical (discrete) variables. Two discrete variables $X$ and $Y$ are said to be **associated** if the distribution of one variable changes (shifts) depending on the level/category of the other. Conversely, they are said to be **independent** when the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X = x_i$ is identical for every category $x_i$ of $X$.

Formally, two attributes (discrete variables) $A$ and $B$ are **independent** if and only if for every cell $(i, j)$ of the contingency table:

$$P(A_i \cap B_j) \;=\; P(A_i)\,P(B_j)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** When $P(A_i \cap B_j) \neq P(A_i)P(B_j)$ for at least one pair $(i,j)$, the attributes are said to be **positively associated** (if the joint probability exceeds the product) or **negatively associated** (if it falls below). This is the cornerstone definition used in KTU board valuation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chocolate shop owner** who keeps a tally of every sale, marking two things on a slip:
1. **Customer Gender** (Male / Female) — Attribute $A$
2. **Chocolate Type Purchased** (Dark / Milk / White) — Attribute $B$

If the owner makes a 2×3 table of these slips, he can ask:

> *"Do males and females buy chocolate in the **same proportions** across the three types?"*

If **yes**, gender and chocolate choice are **independent** — knowing the customer's gender tells you nothing about their likely purchase.
If **no**, then the two attributes are **associated** — knowing the customer's gender *changes* your prediction of what they will buy.

This is precisely what we statistically test using the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test of Independence**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Applications:**
> - **Quality Control:** Is "machine operator" associated with "defect type"?
> - **Medical Research:** Is "treatment category" associated with "patient recovery status"?
> - **Marketing Analytics:** Is "ad-channel" associated with "customer conversion"?
> - **Sociology / Survey Analysis:** Is "income bracket" associated with "education level"?

## 1.3 Types of Two-Variable Discrete Data Structures

| Structure | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **$2 \times 2$ Table** | Both attributes have exactly 2 categories | Gender (M/F) vs Smoking (Yes/No) |
| **$r \times c$ Table** | Attribute $A$ has $r$ categories, $B$ has $c$ categories | Education (4 levels) vs Vote (3 parties) |

The notation throughout KTU textbooks follows the convention:

- $r$ = number of rows (categories of attribute $A$)
- $c$ = number of columns (categories of attribute $B$)
- $n$ = grand total of all observations
- $O_{ij}$ = **Observed Frequency** in row $i$, column $j$
- $E_{ij}$ = **Expected Frequency** in row $i$, column $j$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building the Contingency Table

The fundamental tool for analyzing two discrete variables is the **Contingency Table** (also called a *Cross-Tabulation*).

A general $r \times c$ contingency table has the following structure:

| | $B_1$ | $B_2$ | $\dots$ | $B_c$ | **Row Total** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $A_1$ | $O_{11}$ | $O_{12}$ | $\dots$ | $O_{1c}$ | $R_1$ |
| $A_2$ | $O_{21}$ | $O_{22}$ | $\dots$ | $O_{2c}$ | $R_2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\ddots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $A_r$ | $O_{r1}$ | $O_{r2}$ | $\dots$ | $O_{rc}$ | $R_r$ |
| **Column Total** | $C_1$ | $C_2$ | $\dots$ | $C_c$ | $n$ |

Where:
- $R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{c} O_{ij}$ (row marginal total)
- $C_j = \sum_{i=1}^{r} O_{ij}$ (column marginal total)
- $n = \sum_{i=1}^{r} R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{c} C_j$

## 2.2 Hypothesis Testing for Independence

We test whether $A$ and $B$ are independent using the **Chi-Square Test of Independence**.

**Step 1: State the Hypotheses**
- $H_0$ (Null Hypothesis): The two attributes are **independent** ($O_{ij} = E_{ij}$ for all cells).
- $H_1$ (Alternative Hypothesis): The two attributes are **associated** (at least one $O_{ij} \neq E_{ij}$).

**Step 2: Compute Expected Frequencies**

Under $H_0$ (independence), the expected count in cell $(i,j)$ is:

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \times C_j}{n}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Chi-Square Statistic**

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

**Step 4: Determine Degrees of Freedom**

$$\text{d.f.} = (r - 1)(c - 1)$$

**Step 5: Decision Rule**

Compare $\chi^2_{\text{computed}}$ with the **critical value** $\chi^2_{\alpha,\,(r-1)(c-1)}$ from the chi-square table at significance level $\alpha$ (commonly $\alpha = 0.05$).

- If $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} > \chi^2_{\text{critical}}$ → **Reject $H_0$** → Variables are associated.
- If $\chi^2_{\text{computed}} \leq \chi^2_{\text{critical}}$ → **Accept $H_0$** → Variables are independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?**
> If the variables are truly independent, the observed frequencies should be very close to the expected frequencies (derived from the product of marginals divided by total). The bigger the gap $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2$, the stronger the evidence against independence.

## 2.3 Measures of Association (Strength)

Even when we *reject* $H_0$, the chi-square statistic alone does not tell us **how strong** the association is. For this, KTU 2024 emphasizes the following coefficients:

### 2.3.1 Coefficient of Contingency (Pearson's C)

$$C = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{\chi^2 + n}}$$

- Range: $0 \leq C < 1$
- Maximum possible value: $C_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k-1}{k}}$ where $k = \min(r, c)$
- **Normalized form:** $C_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{C}{C_{\max}}$

### 2.3.2 Phi Coefficient ($\phi$) — for $2 \times 2$ tables only

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n}}$$

- Range: $-1 \leq \phi \leq 1$ (sign conveys direction)
- Strength interpretation:
  - $\vert \phi \vert < 0.3$ → Weak
  - $0.3 \leq \vert \phi \vert < 0.7$ → Moderate
  - $\vert \phi \vert \geq 0.7$ → Strong

### 2.3.3 Cramér's V (Universal — any $r \times c$ table)

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\, c-1)}}$$

- Range: $0 \leq V \leq 1$
- $V = 0$ → perfect independence; $V = 1$ → perfect association
- **This is the most commonly used measure in KTU exams for $r \times c$ tables.**

### 2.3.4 Tschuprow's T

$$T = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n \sqrt{(r-1)(c-1)}}}$$

- Range: $0 \leq T \leq 1$
- Useful when $r = c$ (square tables).

### 2.3.5 Yule's Coefficient of Association ($Q$) — for $2 \times 2$ tables

For a $2 \times 2$ table:
$$
\begin{vmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{vmatrix}
$$

$$Q = \frac{ad - bc}{ad + bc}$$

- Range: $-1 \leq Q \leq 1$
- $|Q| = 1$ means perfect association; $Q = 0$ means independence.

### 2.3.6 Yule's Coefficient of Colligation ($Y$)

$$Y = \frac{\sqrt{ad} - \sqrt{bc}}{\sqrt{ad} + \sqrt{bc}}$$

- Range: $-1 \leq Y \leq 1$

### 2.3.7 Odds Ratio (OR)

$$\text{OR} = \frac{a/b}{c/d} = \frac{ad}{bc}$$

- $\text{OR} = 1$ → Independence
- $\text{OR} > 1$ → Positive association
- $\text{OR} < 1$ → Negative association

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Measure | Formula | Range | Applicable Table |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Expected Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n}$ | — | Any |
| 2 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | $\chi^2 \geq 0$ | Any |
| 3 | Degrees of Freedom | $\text{d.f.} = (r-1)(c-1)$ | — | Any |
| 4 | Pearson's C | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{\chi^2+n}}$ | $[0,\,1)$ | Any |
| 5 | Phi Coefficient | $\phi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n}}$ | $[-1,\,1]$ | $2\times 2$ only |
| 6 | Cramér's V | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n\cdot\min(r-1,c-1)}}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Any |
| 7 | Tschuprow's T | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{\chi^2}{n\sqrt{(r-1)(c-1)}}}$ | $[0,\,1]$ | Any (esp. square) |
| 8 | Yule's Q | $Q = \dfrac{ad-bc}{ad+bc}$ | $[-1,\,1]$ | $2\times 2$ only |
| 9 | Yule's Y | $Y = \dfrac{\sqrt{ad}-\sqrt{bc}}{\sqrt{ad}+\sqrt{bc}}$ | $[-1,\,1]$ | $2\times 2$ only |
| 10 | Odds Ratio | $\text{OR} = \dfrac{ad}{bc}$ | $(0,\infty)$ | $2\times 2$ only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "measure the strength of association," **Cramér's V** is the safest and most universally accepted answer for any $r \times c$ table. For $2 \times 2$ tables, the $\phi$ coefficient or Yule's $Q$ is expected.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Expected Frequency Formula

We start from the independence assumption. Under $H_0$, the probability that an observation falls in cell $(i,j)$ equals the product of its marginal probabilities:

$$P(A_i \cap B_j) = P(A_i) \cdot P(B_j)$$

The **empirical probabilities** (sample estimates) are:

$$\hat{P}(A_i) = \frac{R_i}{n}, \qquad \hat{P}(B_j) = \frac{C_j}{n}$$

Therefore, under independence, the expected proportion in cell $(i,j)$ is:

$$\hat{P}(A_i \cap B_j) = \frac{R_i}{n} \cdot \frac{C_j}{n} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n^2}$$

Multiplying by $n$ to convert proportion to count:

$$E_{ij} = n \cdot \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n^2} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Chi-Square Statistic (Pearson, 1900)

Pearson proposed that the deviation between observed and expected frequencies, normalized by the expected frequency, should follow a chi-square distribution under $H_0$. For each cell, define the standardized residual:

$$z_{ij} = \frac{O_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}}$$

Squaring and summing across all $n_{cells} = r \cdot c$ cells gives:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c} \left( \frac{O_{ij} - E_{ij}}{\sqrt{E_{ij}}} \right)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

Pearson proved (and Fisher later rigorously established via the multinomial distribution) that under $H_0$, $\chi^2 \sim \chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}$ asymptotically.

$$\boxed{\,\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}\,\sim\,\chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}\,}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, $3 \times 3$ Table)

> **Problem:** A survey classifies 600 employees by **Department** (Engineering, Marketing, HR) and **Job Satisfaction** (High, Medium, Low). Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ whether the two attributes are associated.

**Observed Frequencies ($O_{ij}$):**

| Dept \ Satisfaction | High | Medium | Low | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Engineering | 110 | 80 | 30 | 220 |
| Marketing | 60 | 90 | 50 | 200 |
| HR | 30 | 80 | 70 | 180 |
| **Total** | 200 | 250 | 150 | 600 |

**Step 1: Compute Expected Frequencies**

$$E_{ij} = \frac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n}$$

- $E_{11} = \dfrac{220 \times 200}{600} = \dfrac{44000}{600} = 73.33$
- $E_{12} = \dfrac{220 \times 250}{600} = \dfrac{55000}{600} = 91.67$
- $E_{13} = \dfrac{220 \times 150}{600} = \dfrac{33000}{600} = 55.00$
- $E_{21} = \dfrac{200 \times 200}{600} = \dfrac{40000}{600} = 66.67$
- $E_{22} = \dfrac{200 \times 250}{600} = \dfrac{50000}{600} = 83.33$
- $E_{23} = \dfrac{200 \times 150}{600} = \dfrac{30000}{600} = 50.00$
- $E_{31} = \dfrac{180 \times 200}{600} = \dfrac{36000}{600} = 60.00$
- $E_{32} = \dfrac{180 \times 250}{600} = \dfrac{45000}{600} = 75.00$
- $E_{33} = \dfrac{180 \times 150}{600} = \dfrac{27000}{600} = 45.00$

**Step 2: Compute $(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2 / E_{ij}$**

| Cell | $O_{ij}$ | $E_{ij}$ | $O_{ij}-E_{ij}$ | $(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2$ | $(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2/E_{ij}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| (1,1) | 110 | 73.33 | 36.67 | 1344.69 | 18.34 |
| (1,2) | 80 | 91.67 | -11.67 | 136.19 | 1.49 |
| (1,3) | 30 | 55.00 | -25.00 | 625.00 | 11.36 |
| (2,1) | 60 | 66.67 | -6.67 | 44.49 | 0.67 |
| (2,2) | 90 | 83.33 | 6.67 | 44.49 | 0.53 |
| (2,3) | 50 | 50.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| (3,1) | 30 | 60.00 | -30.00 | 900.00 | 15.00 |
| (3,2) | 80 | 75.00 | 5.00 | 25.00 | 0.33 |
| (3,3) | 70 | 45.00 | 25.00 | 625.00 | 13.89 |

**Step 3: Sum to get $\chi^2$**

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 18.34 + 1.49 + 11.36 + 0.67 + 0.53 + 0.00 + 15.00 + 0.33 + 13.89$$

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 61.61$$

**Step 4: Degrees of Freedom**

$$\text{d.f.} = (r-1)(c-1) = (3-1)(3-1) = 4$$

**Step 5: Critical Value at $\alpha = 0.05$, d.f. = 4**

From the chi-square table:

$$\chi^2_{0.05,\,4} = 9.488$$

**Step 6: Decision**

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 61.61 \;>\; \chi^2_{\text{critical}} = 9.488$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Reject $H_0$**. Department and Job Satisfaction are **significantly associated**.

**Step 7: Strength using Cramér's V**

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{61.61}{600 \cdot 2}} = \sqrt{0.0513} = 0.227$$

Since $V = 0.227$, the association is **weak to moderate** in magnitude (statistically significant but not very strong).

## 3.4 Worked Example — $2 \times 2$ Table with Yule's Q

> **Problem:** In a study of 200 patients, 120 received a new drug and 80 received a placebo. Among drug recipients, 90 recovered; among placebo recipients, 40 recovered. Compute Phi, Yule's Q, Yule's Y, and Odds Ratio.

**Observed Table:**

|  | Recovered | Not Recovered | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Drug | $a = 90$ | $b = 30$ | 120 |
| Placebo | $c = 40$ | $d = 40$ | 80 |
| **Total** | 130 | 70 | 200 |

**Step 1: Expected Frequencies**

- $E_{11} = \dfrac{120 \times 130}{200} = 78$
- $E_{12} = \dfrac{120 \times 70}{200} = 42$
- $E_{21} = \dfrac{80 \times 130}{200} = 52$
- $E_{22} = \dfrac{80 \times 70}{200} = 28$

**Step 2: Chi-Square**

$$\chi^2 = \frac{(90-78)^2}{78} + \frac{(30-42)^2}{42} + \frac{(40-52)^2}{52} + \frac{(40-28)^2}{28}$$

$$\chi^2 = \frac{144}{78} + \frac{144}{42} + \frac{144}{52} + \frac{144}{28}$$

$$\chi^2 = 1.846 + 3.429 + 2.769 + 5.143 = 13.187$$

**Step 3: Phi Coefficient**

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{13.187}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0659} = 0.257$$

**Step 4: Yule's Q**

$$Q = \frac{ad - bc}{ad + bc} = \frac{(90)(40) - (30)(40)}{(90)(40) + (30)(40)} = \frac{3600 - 1200}{3600 + 1200} = \frac{2400}{4800} = 0.50$$

**Step 5: Yule's Y**

$$Y = \frac{\sqrt{ad} - \sqrt{bc}}{\sqrt{ad} + \sqrt{bc}} = \frac{\sqrt{3600} - \sqrt{1200}}{\sqrt{3600} + \sqrt{1200}} = \frac{60 - 34.64}{60 + 34.64} = \frac{25.36}{94.64} = 0.268$$

**Step 6: Odds Ratio**

$$\text{OR} = \frac{ad}{bc} = \frac{3600}{1200} = 3.0$$

**Interpretation:** The odds of recovery are 3× higher in the drug group compared to the placebo group — a **strong positive association**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram — Chi-Square Test of Independence

The following block diagram summarizes the complete procedural flow that KTU examiners expect students to reproduce for a 14-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Two Discrete Attributes A and B with Observed Counts"] --> nodeB["Construct r x c Contingency Table with Marginals R_i and C_j"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["State Hypotheses H0 Independent vs H1 Associated"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Compute Expected Frequencies using Eij equals Ri times Cj divided by n"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Verify all Eij greater than or equal to 5 rule of thumb"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Compute Chi Square Statistic: sum of Oij minus Eij squared divided by Eij"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Determine degrees of freedom equals r minus 1 times c minus 1"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Look up Critical Value from Chi Square Table at alpha equals 0.05"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Chi Square computed greater than Critical?"}
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeJ["Reject H0: Attributes are Associated"]
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeK["Accept H0: Attributes are Independent"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Measure Strength: Cramers V or Pearson C"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["STOP: No Further Strength Analysis Needed"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["STOP"]
```

## 4.2 Decision Diagram — Which Measure of Association to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["What is the size of your Contingency Table?"] --> node2x2{"Is it a 2 by 2 table?"}
    node2x2 -- YES --> nodePhi["Use Phi Coefficient OR Yules Q OR Odds Ratio"]
    nodePhi --> nodeSign["Phi and Q and Y can be signed Positive or Negative"]
    node2x2 -- NO --> nodeGen{"Is the table square equals r equals c?"}
    nodeGen -- YES --> nodeTschup["Use Tschuprows T"]
    nodeGen -- NO --> nodeCramer["Use Cramers V or Pearsons C"]
    nodeCramer --> nodeNorm["Normalize Pearsons C using Cmax for fair comparison"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Architecture — Components of the Analysis

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"]
        inpA["Attribute A Categorical Data"]
        inpB["Attribute B Categorical Data"]
        inpN["Sample Size n"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["PROCESSING BLOCK"]
        proc1["Tabulation into r by c Matrix"]
        proc2["Marginal Summation"]
        proc3["Expected Frequency Computation"]
        proc4["Chi Square Statistic"]
        proc5["Degrees of Freedom"]
        proc6["Critical Value Lookup"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
        out1["Decision: Independent or Associated"]
        out2["Strength: Cramers V or Phi or Q"]
        out3["Engineering Interpretation"]
    end

    inpA --> proc1
    inpB --> proc1
    inpN --> proc2
    proc1 --> proc2
    proc2 --> proc3
    proc3 --> proc4
    proc3 --> proc5
    proc4 --> out1
    proc5 --> proc6
    proc6 --> out1
    proc4 --> out2
    out1 --> out3
    out2 --> out3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define association between two discrete variables. What does it mean for two attributes to be statistically independent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two discrete variables $A$ and $B$ are said to be **associated** when the distribution of one variable changes with the level/category of the other. Formally, they are **independent** if and only if for every cell $(i,j)$:

$$P(A_i \cap B_j) = P(A_i) \cdot P(B_j)$$

When this condition fails for at least one cell, the variables are associated. Independence implies that knowing the category of $A$ provides **no information** about the category of $B$.

> *(1 mark for definition of association, 1 mark for the independence condition, 1 mark for the interpretation in plain language.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of the Chi-Square statistic. State its formula and degrees of freedom.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) statistic** measures the total squared deviation between observed and expected frequencies, normalized by the expected frequencies:

$$\chi^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{r}\sum_{j=1}^{c}\frac{(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$$

The degrees of freedom is:

$$\text{d.f.} = (r-1)(c-1)$$

Under the null hypothesis of independence, $\chi^2 \sim \chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}$. A large $\chi^2$ value indicates strong evidence against independence.

> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for degrees of freedom + distribution.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

> A researcher classifies 500 patients by **Blood Group** (A, B, O) and **Disease Status** (Affected, Not Affected) to test if disease status is independent of blood group. The observed data are:

| Blood Group \ Status | Affected | Not Affected | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 80 | 120 | 200 |
| B | 100 | 80 | 180 |
| O | 70 | 50 | 120 |
| **Total** | 250 | 250 | 500 |

**(a)** Test the hypothesis of independence at the 5% level of significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the Phi coefficient and Cramér's V. Interpret the strength of association. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

- $H_0$: Blood Group and Disease Status are independent.
- $H_1$: Blood Group and Disease Status are associated.

**Step 2: Expected Frequencies** $E_{ij} = \dfrac{R_i \cdot C_j}{n}$

- $E_{11} = \dfrac{200 \times 250}{500} = 100$
- $E_{12} = \dfrac{200 \times 250}{500} = 100$
- $E_{21} = \dfrac{180 \times 250}{500} = 90$
- $E_{22} = \dfrac{180 \times 250}{500} = 90$
- $E_{31} = \dfrac{120 \times 250}{500} = 60$
- $E_{32} = \dfrac{120 \times 250}{500} = 60$

All $E_{ij} \geq 5$, so the chi-square approximation is valid.

**Step 3: Compute $\chi^2$**

| Cell | $O_{ij}$ | $E_{ij}$ | $(O_{ij}-E_{ij})^2 / E_{ij}$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| (1,1) | 80 | 100 | $\dfrac{(-20)^2}{100} = 4.000$ |
| (1,2) | 120 | 100 | $\dfrac{20^2}{100} = 4.000$ |
| (2,1) | 100 | 90 | $\dfrac{10^2}{90} = 1.111$ |
| (2,2) | 80 | 90 | $\dfrac{(-10)^2}{90} = 1.111$ |
| (3,1) | 70 | 60 | $\dfrac{10^2}{60} = 1.667$ |
| (3,2) | 50 | 60 | $\dfrac{(-10)^2}{60} = 1.667$ |

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 4.000 + 4.000 + 1.111 + 1.111 + 1.667 + 1.667 = 13.556$$

**Step 4: Degrees of Freedom**

$$\text{d.f.} = (3-1)(2-1) = 2$$

**Step 5: Critical Value**

$$\chi^2_{0.05,\,2} = 5.991$$

**Step 6: Decision**

$$\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 13.556 > \chi^2_{\text{crit}} = 5.991 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** At the 5% significance level, Blood Group and Disease Status are **significantly associated**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Phi Coefficient**

Since this is a $3 \times 2$ table, technically $\phi$ is best for $2 \times 2$. However, it can be computed as:

$$\phi = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{13.556}{500}} = \sqrt{0.02711} = 0.1647$$

**Step 2: Cramér's V** (preferred for $3 \times 2$ tables)

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{\chi^2}{n \cdot \min(r-1,\,c-1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{13.556}{500 \cdot \min(2,1)}} = \sqrt{\frac{13.556}{500}} = \sqrt{0.02711} = 0.1647$$

(In this case, $\phi = V$ because $\min(r-1, c-1) = 1$.)

**Step 3: Interpretation**

$V \approx 0.165$ indicates a **weak** but statistically significant association between blood group and disease status. Although the chi-square test rejects independence, the practical strength of the relationship is small.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** (Internal Choice)

> A $2 \times 2$ contingency table records whether 200 customers responded to a marketing campaign:

|  | Responded | Not Responded | Total |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Email Campaign | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| SMS Campaign | 30 | 70 | 100 |
| **Total** | 90 | 110 | 200 |

**(a)** Test at the 1% level whether the response depends on the campaign type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute Yule's coefficient of association $Q$ and the Odds Ratio. Interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Hypotheses**

- $H_0$: Response is independent of campaign type.
- $H_1$: Response depends on campaign type.
