**Step 3: Mean of Differences**

$$\bar{d} = \frac{-5 - 3 - 2 - 4 - 2 - 4 - 4}{7} = \frac{-24}{7} \approx -3.429$$

**Step 4: Standard Deviation of Differences**

Deviations $(d_i - \bar{d})$ and squares:

| $d_i$ | $d_i - \bar{d}$ | $(d_i - \bar{d})^2$ |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| $-5$ | $-1.571$ | $2.469$ |
| $-3$ | $0.429$ | $0.184$ |
| $-2$ | $1.429$ | $2.041$ |
| $-4$ | $-0.571$ | $0.326$ |
| $-2$ | $1.429$ | $2.041$ |
| $-4$ | $-0.571$ | $0.326$ |
| $-4$ | $-0.571$ | $0.326$ |
| **Total** | — | **$7.713$** |

$$s_d = \sqrt{\frac{7.713}{7-1}} = \sqrt{1.2855} \approx 1.134$$

**Step 5: t-Statistic**

$$t = \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d / \sqrt{n}} = \frac{-3.429}{1.134 / \sqrt{7}} = \frac{-3.429}{1.134 / 2.646} = \frac{-3.429}{0.4286} \approx -8.001$$

**Step 6: Critical Value**

$\nu = 7 - 1 = 6$; for two-tailed $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$t_{critical} = \pm 2.447$$

**Step 7: Decision**

$$\vert -8.001 \vert = 8.001 > 2.447 \implies \text{Reject } H_0$$

**Conclusion:** The training program is **statistically significant** in improving coding speed at the 5% level. The negative mean of differences indicates a substantial improvement (since $\text{Before} - \text{After} < 0$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Degrees of Freedom Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Most common error. Paired t-test uses $\nu = n - 1$ where $n$ is the number of *pairs*, NOT the total number of observations. Independent two-sample uses $\nu = n_1 + n_2 - 2$.
> 2. **Two-tailed vs One-tailed (–1 mark):** Default in KTU is two-tailed unless explicitly stated. Check the wording of $H_1$ carefully.
> 3. **Welch vs. Pooled Confusion (–1 mark):** If the problem does not state "equal variances," examiners often expect the **pooled t-test** (standard form) for KTU, unless "unequal variances" is mentioned.
> 4. **Conclusion Wording (–1 mark):** Never say "We **accept** $H_0$." Always say "We **fail to reject** $H_0$." Failure to reject does not mean proof of $H_0$.
> 5. **Missing Units (–0.5 mark):** Always quote the t-statistic and means with appropriate units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for t-Tests:**

- ⭐ The t-test is used when **$n < 30$** AND **$\sigma$ unknown**.
- ⭐ Three variants: **One-Sample**, **Independent Two-Sample**, **Paired**.
- ⭐ For **paired t-test**, always work on the **differences** $d_i = x_{1i} - x_{2i}$.
- ⭐ The t-distribution has **heavier tails** than the normal distribution.
- ⭐ As $\nu \to \infty$, t-distribution $\to$ standard normal $N(0,1)$.
- ⭐ Degrees of freedom: One-sample = $n-1$, Paired = $n-1$, Independent = $n_1 + n_2 - 2$.
- ⭐ Pooled SD formula: $s_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{(n_1-1)s_1^2 + (n_2-1)s_2^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}}$.
- ⭐ Decision rule: Reject $H_0$ if $\vert t_{calc} \vert > t_{critical}$.
- ⭐ Default significance level in KTU problems: **$\alpha = 0.05$**.
- ⭐ Default test type in KTU problems: **Two-tailed**, unless stated otherwise.
- ⭐ Never "accept" $H_0$ — only "fail to reject" $H_0$.
- ⭐ Assumptions: Normality, Independence, Random sampling, Unknown $\sigma$.
- ⭐ The Welch's t-test is used when variances are unequal (rarely tested in basic KTU).
- ⭐ **One mark reserved for clearly stating the conclusion** in plain language context.
- ⭐ **Always show the t-table lookup step** with the correct $\nu$ and $\alpha$.
- ⭐ Common engineering applications: Quality control, A/B testing, before-after experiments, clinical trials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT611 - DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Supervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning in which a model is trained on a **labelled dataset** — i.e., each input vector $\mathbf{x}_i$ is paired with a known target output $y_i$. The model learns the mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ by minimising a loss function $\mathcal{L}(f(\mathbf{x}_i), y_i)$ over the training set.

**Classification** is the sub-branch of supervised learning where the target variable $y$ is **categorical (discrete)** rather than continuous. The output space $\mathcal{Y} = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ consists of a finite set of class labels.

Mathematically, the goal is to learn a **decision function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ that partitions the feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ into $K$ decision regions $\mathcal{R}_1, \mathcal{R}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{R}_K$, such that each region is associated with one class label.

### 1.2 Types of Classification Problems

| Type | Number of Classes | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Classification** | 2 | Spam vs. Not-Spam email |
| **Multiclass Classification** | $>2$, single label | Digit recognition (0–9) |
| **Multilabel Classification** | $>2$, multiple labels per instance | Movie genre tagging |
| **Imbalanced Classification** | 2 (skewed prior) | Fraud detection (1% fraud) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Mail" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a postal worker who has been shown 10,000 previously sorted letters — half marked "Local" and half marked "International". The worker has learned to look at the **postage stamp, return address, and handwriting style** to make a decision. When a *new* letter arrives (unseen during training), the worker uses these learned cues to classify it.
>
> - The **10,000 letters** = training set with labels
> - The **cues (stamp, address)** = features $\mathbf{x}$
> - The **decision "Local/International"** = predicted class $\hat{y}$
> - The **postal worker** = the classifier $f(\mathbf{x})$

### 1.4 Key Terminology Every KTU Student Must Know

> [!NOTE]
> - **Feature Vector** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_d)$: the $d$-dimensional input describing one sample.
> - **Label** $y$: the true class for that sample.
> - **Training Set** $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$: the labelled examples used to fit the model.
> - **Test Set**: held-out labelled data used to evaluate generalisation.
> - **Hypothesis / Classifier** $h_\theta(\mathbf{x})$: the parametrised mapping function.
> - **Decision Boundary**: the surface in feature space where $P(y = C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = P(y = C_j \mid \mathbf{x})$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline (Why & How)

The classification workflow follows a deterministic six-stage pipeline. Every KTU problem statement on "design a classifier" implicitly tests your understanding of this flow.

1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the class set $\mathcal{Y}$ and collect domain knowledge.
2. **Data Acquisition & Labelling** — Gather $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ pairs; labelling is the *expensive* step.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Normalise, encode categorical variables, handle missing values.
4. **Model Selection** — Choose an algorithm family (linear, tree-based, probabilistic, kernel-based).
5. **Training (Parameter Estimation)** — Minimise the empirical risk:
$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\big(h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i),\, y_i\big) + \lambda \, \Omega(\theta)$$
where the first term is the empirical loss and $\lambda \Omega(\theta)$ is a regulariser.
6. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Predict on unseen test data and report metrics.

### 2.2 Foundational Algorithms — The "How" Behind Each

#### A. Logistic Regression
Despite the name, it is a **classification** algorithm. It models the posterior probability using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:
$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}, \quad z = \theta^\top \mathbf{x}$$
The decision boundary is linear in $\mathbf{x}$ at $P = 0.5$.

#### B. k-Nearest Neighbours (k-NN)
A **lazy, non-parametric** learner. For a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$, it finds the $k$ closest training points (by Euclidean distance) and assigns the majority class:
$$\hat{y} = \mathrm{mode}\big(\{y_i : \mathbf{x}_i \in \mathcal{N}_k(\mathbf{x}_q)\}\big)$$

#### C. Decision Tree
Recursively partitions the feature space using axis-aligned splits. At each node, it chooses the feature $x_j$ and threshold $t$ that maximise **Information Gain** (equivalently, minimise Gini impurity or entropy).

#### D. Naive Bayes
Applies Bayes' theorem with a **strong (naive) independence assumption**:
$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

#### E. Support Vector Machine (SVM)
Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** $\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ separating the two classes. Solved as:
$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \tfrac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula KTU examiners can throw at you for the topic **Supervised Learning – Classification**. Memorise the units and dimensions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid (Logistic) Function | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | $z \in \mathbb{R}$, $\sigma \in (0,1)$ | Derivative: $\sigma'(z)=\sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ |
| 2 | Logistic Loss (Cross-Entropy) | $\mathcal{L} = -\big[y\log(\hat{p}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})\big]$ | $\hat{p} = P(y=1\mid\mathbf{x})$ | Used per-sample |
| 3 | Logistic Cost (averaged) | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{N}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[y_i\log\hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\big]$ | $N$ samples | Convex in $\theta$ |
| 4 | Euclidean Distance (k-NN) | $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{d}(x_{im}-x_{jm})^{2}}$ | $d$ features | Most common metric |
| 5 | Manhattan Distance (k-NN) | $d_{L1} = \displaystyle\sum_{m=1}^{d} \vert x_{im} - x_{jm} \vert$ |  | Use `\vert` for absolute value in written work |
| 6 | Gini Impurity | $G = 1 - \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k^2$ | $p_k$ = proportion of class $k$ | Used in CART decision trees |
| 7 | Entropy | $H = -\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{K} p_k \log_2 p_k$ | bits | Used in ID3/C4.5 |
| 8 | Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \displaystyle\sum_{v} \dfrac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $S_v$ = subset on value $v$ | Splitting criterion |
| 9 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+FP+TN+FN}$ | Range $[0,1]$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| 10 | Precision | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | "Of predicted positives, how many correct" |  |
| 11 | Recall (Sensitivity, TPR) | $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | "Of actual positives, how many caught" |  |
| 12 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}}$ | Harmonic mean | Penalises extremes |
| 13 | Specificity (TNR) | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ |  |  |
| 14 | False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Spec}$ |  | x-axis of ROC |
| 15 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(y\mid\mathbf{x}) = \dfrac{P(\mathbf{x}\mid y)P(y)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ |  | Foundation of Naive Bayes |
| 16 | SVM Margin | $\text{margin} = \dfrac{2}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$ |  | Maximising margin $\Leftrightarrow$ minimising $\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert$ |
| 17 | Naive Bayes Posterior | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y}\, P(y) \displaystyle\prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j\mid y)$ | MAP estimate |  |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Classifying tumours as malignant/benign from imaging features (SVM, Deep CNN).
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection using imbalanced classifiers with SMOTE + Random Forest.
- **NLP:** Spam filtering using Multinomial Naive Bayes on TF-IDF features.
- **Manufacturing:** Defect classification on production-line sensor data using k-NN.
- **Biometrics:** Face recognition pipelines often end with an SVM classifier on embedding features.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Sigmoid Function from Log-Odds

**Starting point:** We want a function that maps the linear combination $z = \theta^\top \mathbf{x} \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ into a probability $p \in (0, 1)$.

**Step 1 — Define the log-odds (logit):**
$$\log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = z = \theta^\top \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 2 — Exponentiate both sides:**
$$\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{z}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $p$:**
$$p = (1-p) e^{z} \;\Longrightarrow\; p = e^{z} - p\, e^{z}$$

**Step 4 — Collect the $p$ terms:**
$$p + p\, e^{z} = e^{z} \;\Longrightarrow\; p(1 + e^{z}) = e^{z}$$

**Step 5 — Final closed form:**
$$p = \frac{e^{z}}{1 + e^{z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \sigma(z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly the **sigmoid (logistic) function**. The same derivation appears verbatim in the Bishop *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* textbook, Chapter 4.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gradient of the Logistic Cross-Entropy Cost

**Goal:** Compute $\dfrac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$ where:
$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\Big[y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)\Big], \quad \hat{p}_i = \sigma(\theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i)$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate a single sample's loss with respect to $z_i = \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i$.**

Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ and the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial z_i} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial \hat{p}_i} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i}$$

Compute the two pieces separately.

**Step 2 — Partial derivative of $\mathcal{L}_i$ w.r.t. $\hat{p}_i$:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial \hat{p}_i} = -\left(\frac{y_i}{\hat{p}_i} - \frac{1-y_i}{1-\hat{p}_i}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative of $\hat{p}_i$ w.r.t. $z_i$:**
$$\frac{\partial \hat{p}_i}{\partial z_i} = \hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)$$

**Step 4 — Multiply them:**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial z_i} = -\left(\frac{y_i}{\hat{p}_i} - \frac{1-y_i}{1-\hat{p}_i}\right) \cdot \hat{p}_i(1-\hat{p}_i)$$

Simplify algebraically:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_i}{\partial z_i} = -\Big[y_i(1-\hat{p}_i) - (1-y_i)\hat{p}_i\Big] = -\big[y_i - y_i\hat{p}_i - \hat{p}_i + y_i\hat{p}_i\big] = -(y_i - \hat{p}_i) = \hat{p}_i - y_i$$

**Step 5 — Apply the chain rule once more to get the gradient w.r.t. $\theta_j$:**

Since $z_i = \theta^\top \mathbf{x}_i$, we have $\dfrac{\partial z_i}{\partial \theta_j} = x_{ij}$. Therefore:
$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\, x_{ij}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact vectorised form:**
$$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{X}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{y})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This gradient is what Gradient Descent uses to update $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla_\theta J(\theta)$. Note the elegant form — the gradient is **the average of (prediction − truth) × input**, which is identical in structure to the linear-regression gradient but with a non-linear $\hat{p}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Confusion Matrix and All Metrics

**Problem.** A binary classifier is evaluated on a test set of **1000** samples. The confusion matrix is:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP = 180 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | FP = 50 | TN = 750 |

**Step 1 — Verify totals:**
- Total positives: $180 + 20 = 200$
- Total negatives: $50 + 750 = 800$
- Total: $200 + 800 = 1000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Compute Accuracy:**
$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = \frac{180 + 750}{1000} = \frac{930}{1000} = 0.93 \;(93\%)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Precision:**
$$\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 50} = \frac{180}{230} \approx 0.7826 \;(78.26\%)$$

**Step 4 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity):**
$$\text{Rec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \;(90\%)$$

**Step 5 — Compute F1-Score:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Rec}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Rec}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.7826 \cdot 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90} = \frac{1.4087}{1.6826} \approx 0.8373$$

**Step 6 — Compute Specificity:**
$$\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{750}{750 + 50} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375$$

**Step 7 — Compute False Positive Rate:**
$$FPR = 1 - \text{Spec} = 1 - 0.9375 = 0.0625$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation:**
> [!NOTE]
> The accuracy is high (93%) but the F1 is moderate (84%) because the class is mildly imbalanced (20% positive). On a *truly* imbalanced dataset (e.g., 1% positive), accuracy becomes meaningless and **F1 / ROC-AUC** must be reported.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection Cheat-Sheet

| Algorithm | Best For | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logistic Regression** | Linearly separable data, baseline | Fast, interpretable, probabilistic | Limited on non-linear data |
| **k-NN** | Small datasets, non-linear boundaries | Zero training time, intuitive | Slow at inference, curse of dimensionality |
| **Decision Tree** | Mixed feature types, explainability | Highly interpretable, no scaling needed | Prone to overfitting |
| **Naive Bayes** | Text classification, high-dim sparse | Extremely fast, works with little data | Independence assumption is often false |
| **SVM (RBF kernel)** | Medium-sized, non-linear | Strong on high-dim, robust to outliers | Slow on large $N$, no probability output natively |
| **Random Forest** | Tabular data, general purpose | Robust, handles missing data, gives feature importance | Less interpretable, large model size |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Supervised Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Labelled Dataset] --> A1[Step 1: Data Cleaning]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2: Feature Engineering and Encoding]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Train Validation Test Split]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Model Training on Train Set]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Hyperparameter Tuning on Validation Set]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Final Evaluation on Test Set]
    A6 --> A7{Performance Acceptable}
    A7 -- Yes --> A8[Deploy Model]
    A7 -- No --> A9[Iterate: Try New Algorithm or Features]
    A9 --> A1
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Classification Algorithms] --> B1[Linear Models]
    B0 --> B2[Instance Based]

    B1 --> B1b[Linear SVM]

    B3 --> B3b[Random Forest]

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Confusion Matrix Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C0[Confusion Matrix 2x2] --> C1[Row 1: Actual Positive]
    C0 --> C2[Row 2: Actual Negative]
    C1 --> C1a[TP: Predicted Positive and Correct]
    C1 --> C1b[FN: Predicted Negative but Actually Positive]
    C2 --> C2a[FP: Predicted Positive but Actually Negative]
    C2 --> C2b[TN: Predicted Negative and Correct]
    C1a --> D0[Metric: Precision and Recall derive from TP]
    C1b --> D0
    C2a --> D1[Metric: Specificity derives from TN and FP]
    C2b --> D1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Logistic Regression Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Input Feature Vector x] --> F1[Compute Linear Score z equals theta transpose x]
    F1 --> F2[Apply Sigmoid sigma z]
    F2 --> F3{p greater than or equal to 0.5}
    F3 -- Yes --> F4[Predict Class 1 Positive]
    F3 -- No --> F5[Predict Class 0 Negative]
    F4 --> F6[Decision Boundary: theta transpose x equals 0]
    F5 --> F6
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cross-Validation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[Full Labelled Dataset D] --> G1[Partition into K Folds]
    G1 --> G2[Fold 1 held out as Validation]
    G1 --> G3[Fold 2 held out as Validation]
    G1 --> G4[Fold 3 held out as Validation]
    G1 --> G5[Fold K held out as Validation]
    G2 --> G2a[Train on K minus 1 folds, evaluate on Fold 1]
    G3 --> G3a[Train on K minus 1 folds, evaluate on Fold 2]
    G4 --> G4a[Train on K minus 1 folds, evaluate on Fold 3]
    G5 --> G5a[Train on K minus 1 folds, evaluate on Fold K]
    G2a --> H0[Average the K validation scores]
    G3a --> H0
    G4a --> H0
    G5a --> H0
    H0 --> H1[Final Reported CV Score with Confidence Interval]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Labels** | Uses labelled data $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ | Uses unlabelled data $\{\mathbf{x}_i\}$ |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $\mathbf{x} \to y$ | Discover hidden structure / clusters |
| **Example** | Email spam classification (Logistic Regression) | Customer segmentation (K-Means) |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, ROC-AUC | Silhouette score, inertia |

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for evaluation comparison.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a confusion matrix? Explain the terms Precision, Recall, and F1-score with formulas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **confusion matrix** is a $2 \times 2$ table (for binary classification) that tabulates the counts of correct and incorrect predictions against actual labels:
- **TP** (True Positive), **FP** (False Positive), **TN** (True Negative), **FN** (False Negative).

The metrics are:

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

Precision answers *"Of those predicted positive, how many are truly positive?"* while Recall answers *"Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?"* F1 is their harmonic mean.

*(Award 1 mark for confusion matrix definition, 1 mark for Precision/Recall formulas, 1 mark for F1 formula and explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Logistic Regression** algorithm in detail. Derive the cost function and the gradient descent update rule. State the assumptions of the algorithm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the following training data for a binary classifier (Class +1 or −1), compute the **Euclidean distance** from the query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$ to all points, and predict the class using **k-NN with k = 3**. Use majority voting.

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Label $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 2 | +1 |
| P2 | 2 | 3 | +1 |
| P3 | 3 | 5 | +1 |
| P4 | 6 | 8 | −1 |
| P5 | 7 | 9 | −1 |
| P6 | 8 | 8 | −1 |

**(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a): Logistic Regression — Full Derivation (7 marks)**

**1. Algorithm Description (2 marks)**
Logistic regression is a **discriminative, parametric, linear classifier** that models $P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta)$ using the sigmoid:
$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \theta) = \sigma(\theta^\top \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^\top \mathbf{x}}}$$

**2. Cost Function Derivation (3 marks)**
We use the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** principle. The likelihood over $N$ i.i.d. samples is:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{p}_i^{y_i} (1-\hat{p}_i)^{1-y_i}$$
Take the log-likelihood:
$$\ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i) \Big]$$

Maximising $\ell(\theta)$ is equivalent to minimising the **cross-entropy cost**:
$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Big[ y_i \log \hat{p}_i + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{p}_i) \Big]$$

**3. Gradient Descent Update (2 marks)**
From the derivation in Section 3.2, the gradient is:
$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\, x_{ij}$$

The gradient descent update rule with learning rate $\eta$ is:
$$\theta_j \leftarrow \theta_j - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(\hat{p}_i - y_i)\, x_{ij}$$

In vector form:
$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{X}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The classes are **linearly separable in the logit space**.
2. Observations are **independent** (no autocorrelation).
3. Features are **not highly correlated** (no severe multicollinearity).
4. **Large sample size** for stable MLE estimates.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b): k-NN Prediction (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute Euclidean distances from $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$ to each point using:
$$d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{(x_{q1}-x_{i1})^2 + (x_{q2}-x_{i2})^2}$$

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Label | Calculation | Distance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | 1 | 2 | +1 | $\sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-2)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8}$ | **2.828** |
| P2 | 2 | 3 | +1 | $\sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2}$ | **1.414** |
| P3 | 3 | 5 | +1 | $\sqrt{(3-3)^2 + (4-5)^2} = \sqrt{0+1} = 1$ | **1.000** |
| P4 | 6 | 8 | −1 | $\sqrt{(3-6)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+16} = \sqrt{25}$ | **5.000** |
| P5 | 7 | 9 | −1 | $\sqrt{(3-7)^2 + (4-9)^2} = \sqrt{16+25} = \sqrt{41}$ | **6.403** |
| P6 | 8 | 8 | −1 | $\sqrt{(3-8)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{25+16} = \sqrt{41}$ | **6.403** |

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sort by ascending distance and take the top $k = 3$:

| Rank | Point | Distance | Label |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P3 | 1.000 | +1 |
| 2 | P2 | 1.414 | +1 |
| 3 | P1 | 2.828 | +1 |

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply majority voting: 3 votes for $+1$, 0 votes for $-1$.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Final Prediction: $\hat{y} = +1$.

**Conclusion:** The query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (3, 4)$ is classified as belonging to **Class +1** with 100% confidence among the 3 nearest neighbours.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Naive Bayes Classifier** with the assumption of conditional independence. Derive the MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) decision rule. For a text-classification problem with vocabulary $\{w_1, w_2, w_3\}$ and two classes (Spam, Ham), given:
- $P(\text{Spam}) = 0.4$, $P(\text{Ham}) = 0.6$
- $P(w_1 \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.5$, $P(w_2 \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.3$, $P(w_3 \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.1$
- $P(w_1 \mid \text{Ham}) = 0.2$, $P(w_2 \mid \text{Ham}) = 0.4$, $P(w_3 \mid \text{Ham}) = 0.1$

Classify the email containing words $\{w_1, w_2\}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hospital uses a classifier to detect a rare disease. Out of **10,000** patients, only **100** have the disease. The classifier identifies **80** of the diseased patients correctly, and produces **200** false positives. Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and the False Negative Rate. Comment on whether Accuracy is a good metric here. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a): Naive Bayes (7 marks)**

**1. Theoretical Foundation (3 marks)**
Naive Bayes applies Bayes' theorem with a **conditional independence assumption** — given the class $y$, the features $x_1, \ldots, x_d$ are assumed mutually independent. The posterior is:
$$P(y \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

The **MAP decision rule** is:
$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{y \in \mathcal{Y}} \; P(y) \prod_{j=1}^{d} P(x_j \mid y)$$

**2. Numerical Computation (4 marks)**

For **Spam**:
$$P(\text{Spam}) \cdot P(w_1 \mid \text{Spam}) \cdot P(w_2 \mid \text{Spam}) = 0.4 \times 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.0600$$

For **Ham**:
$$P(\text{Ham}) \cdot P(w_1 \mid \text{Ham}) \cdot P(w_2 \mid \text{Ham}) = 0.6 \times 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.0480$$

Comparison: $0.0600 > 0.0480$, therefore $\hat{y} = \text{Spam}$.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): Medical Classifier Evaluation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Set up the confusion matrix. Given:
- Total diseased = 100, Total healthy = 9900
- TP = 80 (correctly identified diseased)
- FN = 100 − 80 = **20** (missed diseased)
- FP = **200** (healthy wrongly flagged)
- TN = 9900 − 200 = **9700**

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP = 80 | FN = 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | FP = 200 | TN = 9700 |

**Step 2 (1 mark):**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{80 + 9700}{10000} = \frac{9780}{10000} = 0.9780 \;(97.80\%)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):**
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{80}{80 + 200} = \frac{80}{280} \approx 0.2857 \;(28.57\%)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):**
$$\text{Recall} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \;(80\%)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.2857 \cdot 0.80}{0.2857 + 0.80} = \frac{0.4571}{1.0857} \approx 0.4211$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):**
$$\text{FNR} = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20 \;(20\%)$$

**Comment (Bonus insight):**
> [!WARNING]
> Although the accuracy is 97.80% (impressive on the surface), the **Precision is only 28.57%** — meaning nearly 3 out of every 4 patients flagged as diseased are actually healthy. This causes **unnecessary stress, follow-up tests, and clinical costs**. In medical diagnosis, **Recall and F1-score** (or ROC-AUC) are far more appropriate than accuracy, especially under class imbalance.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing marks in the KTU Valuation Hall:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision and Recall.** Memorise: *Precision = "of predicted positive, how correct?"*; *Recall = "of actual positive, how caught?"*
> 2. **Using Accuracy on imbalanced data.** Always check the class distribution first. A 99% accuracy can be achieved on a 99:1 imbalanced dataset by predicting the majority class always — this is *useless*.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign in the cross-entropy cost.** The cost $J(\theta)$ is the **negative log-likelihood**; many students drop the sign and report a maximisation problem.
> 4. **Not standardising features for k-NN and SVM.** k-NN uses Euclidean distance, so features with larger scales will dominate. Always apply StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler.
> 5. **Mixing up Gini impurity and Entropy.** Gini = $1 - \sum p_k^2$; Entropy = $-\sum p_k \log_2 p_k$. Both are valid but produce different trees.
> 6. **In k-NN, choosing $k$ without justification.** $k$ too small → overfitting (high variance); $k$ too large → underfitting (high bias). Use cross-validation.
> 7. **Treating Logistic Regression as a regression model.** Despite the name, it is strictly a **classification** algorithm — a common trap question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Supervised Learning: Classification**

- **Definition:** Learning $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ where $\mathcal{Y}$ is discrete (class labels), from labelled data.
- **Two main flavours:** Binary (2 classes) vs. Multiclass ($K > 2$).
- **Algorithm families:** Linear (Logistic Regression, Linear SVM), Instance-based (k-NN), Tree-based (Decision Tree, Random Forest), Probabilistic (Naive Bayes), Kernel (SVM with RBF).
- **Sigmoid function:** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ maps any real number to $(0, 1)$.
- **Logistic loss per sample:** $\mathcal{L} = -[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$.
- **Logistic gradient (vector):** $\nabla_\theta J = \dfrac{1}{N} \mathbf{X}^\top (\hat{\mathbf{p}} - \mathbf{y})$.
- **k-NN:** Lazy learner; majority vote among $k$ nearest neighbours. Watch the **distance metric** and **feature scaling**.
- **Naive Bayes MAP rule:** $\hat{y} = \arg\max_y P(y) \prod_j P(x_j \mid y)$. Strong independence assumption.
- **SVM:** Maximises the margin $\dfrac{2}{\lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert}$. Kernel trick handles non-linearity.
- **Decision Tree:** Splits on Information Gain (entropy-based) or Gini reduction.
- **Confusion matrix:** 2×2 table of TP, FP, TN, FN. Foundation of all classification metrics.
- **Accuracy** = $\dfrac{TP+TN}{\text{Total}}$. **Inappropriate on imbalanced data.**
- **Precision** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$. **Recall** = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$. **F1** = harmonic mean of the two.
- **ROC curve:** TPR (y-axis) vs. FPR (x-axis) at varying thresholds. **AUC** = area under the ROC; closer to 1 is better.
- **Overfitting vs. Underfitting:** High variance (overfit) → reduce model complexity / more data / regularisation. High bias (underfit) → increase complexity / add features.
- **Train/Validation/Test split** + **K-fold Cross-Validation** is the gold standard for honest evaluation.
- **Preprocessing is mandatory:** Handle missing values, encode categoricals (one-hot), scale numerics (StandardScaler).
- **Always justify the metric:** Accuracy for balanced problems; F1/ROC-AUC for imbalanced; Precision when false positives are costly (spam filter); Recall when false negatives are costly (cancer detection).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT611 - DATA ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Naive Bayes** is a family of **probabilistic supervised learning algorithms** based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a **strong (naive) assumption of conditional independence** between every pair of features given the value of the class label. It is primarily deployed for **classification tasks** in data analytics, text mining, and pattern recognition pipelines.

Mathematically, given a feature vector $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a class variable $C \in \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$, Naive Bayes selects the class that **maximizes the posterior probability**:

$$C_{MAP} = \arg\max_{c \in C} P(c \mid \mathbf{X})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Naive Bayes is a **generative probabilistic model** — it models the joint distribution $P(X, C)$ rather than directly modeling $P(C \mid X)$ as logistic regression does. The "naive" prefix comes from the simplifying assumption that features are mutually independent given the class.

## 1.2 Intuition: The Doctor's Heuristic Analogy

Imagine a doctor who is trying to diagnose whether a patient has the flu based on three observed symptoms: fever, body ache, and cough. The doctor reasons **naively** by treating each symptom as an **independent clue**, even though in reality they are correlated.

The doctor thinks:
- *"I know the base rate of flu in the community (prior)."*
- *"I know how often a flu patient has a fever, how often they have body ache, how often they cough (likelihoods)."*
- *"Multiplying these independent clues with the prior tells me how likely flu is (posterior)."*

That is exactly how Naive Bayes operates — it combines the **prior knowledge** about a class with **independent evidence** from each feature to reach a final decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Map (Examiner's Lingo):**
> - **Prior Probability** $P(C)$ — Belief *before* seeing the data.
> - **Likelihood** $P(X \mid C)$ — How probable the data is under each class.
> - **Evidence** $P(X)$ — Normalizing constant (often dropped in comparison).
> - **Posterior Probability** $P(C \mid X)$ — Updated belief *after* seeing the data.
> - **MAP Estimate** — Maximum A Posteriori class decision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bayes' Theorem — The Foundation

The entire Naive Bayes framework rests on **Bayes' Theorem** of conditional probability:

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}$$

For a multi-feature vector $\mathbf{X} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, the joint likelihood $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C)$ becomes intractable in general. The **naive assumption** factorizes it into a product of univariate likelihoods:

$$P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

Thus, the posterior simplifies to:

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) \propto P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

## 2.2 Why Does It Work? (The "Why" and "How")

- **Why independence assumption?** It makes the computation of $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C)$ linear in the number of features $n$ instead of exponential $2^n$. This makes Naive Bayes extremely fast and scalable.
- **Why does it still perform well despite the assumption being violated in real data?** Because the model only needs the **rank ordering** of posteriors to be correct for classification; the absolute magnitudes can be wildly inaccurate.
- **How is the decision made?** Pick the class with the **highest posterior** — i.e., the **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori)** decision rule.

## 2.3 The Zero-Frequency Problem & Laplace Smoothing

If a categorical feature value never co-occurs with a class in the training set, its likelihood becomes **zero**, which collapses the entire product to zero. **Laplace (add-$\alpha$) smoothing** solves this:

$$\hat{P}(x_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}(c) + \alpha \cdot V}$$

where $V$ = vocabulary size (number of unique feature values), and $\alpha \ge 0$ is the smoothing parameter (typically $\alpha = 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Laplace smoothing is a **mandatory concept** in KTU exams when the question involves a small training set — examiners almost always test whether the student knows to add $\alpha$ to numerator and $\alpha V$ to denominator.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Glossary |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Bayes' Theorem** | $P(C \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | $P(C \mid X)$ = posterior, $P(X \mid C)$ = likelihood, $P(C)$ = prior, $P(X)$ = evidence |
| 2 | **Naive Independence Factorization** | $P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | $n$ = number of features, $x_i$ = $i$-th feature value |
| 3 | **Posterior (proportional form)** | $P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) \propto P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Drop evidence $P(X)$ for comparison |
| 4 | **MAP Decision Rule** | $\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c} P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | $\hat{c}$ = predicted class |
| 5 | **Prior Probability (Categorical)** | $P(c) = \dfrac{N_c}{N}$ | $N_c$ = samples in class $c$, $N$ = total samples |
| 6 | **Likelihood (Categorical)** | $P(x_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i, c)}{\text{count}(c)}$ | Counts from training data |
| 7 | **Laplace-Smoothed Likelihood** | $\hat{P}(x_i \mid c) = \dfrac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}(c) + \alpha \cdot V}$ | $V$ = no. of unique values, $\alpha$ = smoothing |
| 8 | **Gaussian Likelihood (continuous)** | $P(x_i \mid c) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_c^2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x_i - \mu_c)^2}{2\sigma_c^2}\right)$ | $\mu_c, \sigma_c^2$ = class mean and variance |
| 9 | **Log-Posterior Trick** | $\log P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \log P(C) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C)$ | Avoids floating-point underflow |
| 10 | **Bernoulli Likelihood (binary)** | $P(x_i \mid c) = p_i^{x_i} (1 - p_i)^{1 - x_i}$ | $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$, $p_i = P(x_i = 1 \mid c)$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spam Filtering** (Gmail, Outlook use a Naive Bayes layer in their spam classifiers).
- **Sentiment Analysis** of product reviews and social-media feeds.
- **Medical Diagnosis** — screening diseases from symptom checklists.
- **Document Categorization** — news article tagging, legal text classification.
- **Real-time Recommendation Engines** and **Intrusion Detection Systems** in networks.
- **Weather Prediction**, **Credit Risk Scoring**, and **Fault Diagnosis** in IoT pipelines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to Naive Bayes Posterior

We start with Bayes' theorem applied to a class $C$ and feature vector $\mathbf{X}$:

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}$$

**Step 1 — Expand the joint likelihood using the chain rule of probability:**

$$P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C) = P(x_1 \mid C) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C) \cdot P(x_3 \mid x_1, x_2, C) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the naive (conditional independence) assumption.** The assumption states:

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C) = P(x_i \mid C)$$

In words: *each feature is conditionally independent of every other feature given the class label.* Substituting back:

$$P(\mathbf{X} \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Bayes' theorem:**

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) = \frac{P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)}{P(\mathbf{X})}$$

**Step 4 — Drop the evidence term for classification (since $P(\mathbf{X})$ is constant across classes):**

$$P(C \mid \mathbf{X}) \propto P(C) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$$

**Step 5 — Final MAP decision rule:**

$$\hat{c} = \arg\max_{c \in C} \left[ P(c) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Insight:** The factorized form reduces complexity from $O(2^n)$ to $O(n)$ parameter estimation — a massive win for high-dimensional data such as text corpora where $n$ may exceed $10^5$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Classic KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**

A training set of **5 email documents** is provided, each labeled as either **Spam (S)** or **Not Spam (N)**. Each document is represented by the presence/absence of three words: $w_1 = \text{``buy"}$, $w_2 = \text{``free"}$, $w_3 = \text{``meeting"}$. The class of each document is shown in the table below (✓ = word present):

| Doc ID | $w_1$ (buy) | $w_2$ (free) | $w_3$ (meeting) | Class |
|--------|-------------|--------------|------------------|--------|
| $d_1$  | ✓           | ✓            | ✗                | Spam   |
| $d_2$  | ✗           | ✓            | ✗                | Spam   |
| $d_3$  | ✗           | ✗            | ✓                | Not Spam |
| $d_4$  | ✓           | ✗            | ✓                | Not Spam |
| $d_5$  | ✗           | ✗            | ✓                | Not Spam |

A new test document $d_{test}$ has the feature vector: $w_1 = $ ✓ (buy), $w_2 = $ ✓ (free), $w_3 = $ ✓ (meeting).

**Classify $d_{test}$ using the Naive Bayes classifier with Laplace smoothing $\alpha = 1$ and vocabulary size $V = 3$.**

### Step 1: Compute Prior Probabilities

From the training set: 2 documents are Spam, 3 are Not Spam.

$$P(S) = \frac{2}{5}, \quad P(N) = \frac{3}{5}$$

### Step 2: Count Feature Occurrences per Class

For the **Spam** class (total word occurrences = 2 documents with 1 word each in $d_1$ + 1 word in $d_2$ = wait, recount carefully).

Let us recount word counts per class. Each document is treated as a bag of its present words (i.e., the count is the number of documents in class $c$ that contain word $w_i$):

- **Spam class (2 docs):**
  - $w_1$ (buy) appears in $d_1$ only → count = **1**
  - $w_2$ (free) appears in $d_1, d_2$ → count = **2**
  - $w_3$ (meeting) appears in 0 docs → count = **0**
  - Total word-presence slots in Spam = 1 + 2 + 0 = **3** (i.e., $|D_c| = 3$)

- **Not Spam class (3 docs):**
  - $w_1$ (buy) appears in $d_4$ → count = **1**
  - $w_2$ (free) appears in 0 docs → count = **0**
  - $w_3$ (meeting) appears in $d_3, d_4, d_5$ → count = **3**
  - Total word-presence slots in Not Spam = 1 + 0 + 3 = **4**

### Step 3: Apply Laplace Smoothing ($\alpha = 1$, $V = 3$)

The smoothed likelihood formula is:

$$\hat{P}(w_i \mid c) = \frac{\text{count}(w_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}_c + \alpha \cdot V}$$

For **Spam** class:
- $\hat{P}(w_1 \mid S) = \dfrac{1 + 1}{3 + 1 \cdot 3} = \dfrac{2}{6} = \dfrac{1}{3}$
- $\hat{P}(w_2 \mid S) = \dfrac{2 + 1}{3 + 3} = \dfrac{3}{6} = \dfrac{1}{2}$
- $\hat{P}(w_3 \mid S) = \dfrac{0 + 1}{3 + 3} = \dfrac{1}{6}$

For **Not Spam** class:
- $\hat{P}(w_1 \mid N) = \dfrac{1 + 1}{4 + 3} = \dfrac{2}{7}$
- $\hat{P}(w_2 \mid N) = \dfrac{0 + 1}{4 + 3} = \dfrac{1}{7}$
- $\hat{P}(w_3 \mid N) = \dfrac{3 + 1}{4 + 3} = \dfrac{4}{7}$

### Step 4: Compute Posterior Scores for $d_{test}$ (All three words present)

**Spam score:**

$$P(S \mid d_{test}) \propto P(S) \cdot \hat{P}(w_1 \mid S) \cdot \hat{P}(w_2 \mid S) \cdot \hat{P}(w_3 \mid S)$$

$$= \frac{2}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{6} = \frac{2}{180} = \frac{1}{90}$$

**Not Spam score:**

$$P(N \mid d_{test}) \propto P(N) \cdot \hat{P}(w_1 \mid N) \cdot \hat{P}(w_2 \mid N) \cdot \hat{P}(w_3 \mid N)$$

$$= \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{1}{7} \cdot \frac{4}{7} = \frac{3 \cdot 8}{5 \cdot 343} = \frac{24}{1715}$$

### Step 5: Compare and Decide

Convert both scores to a common denominator (LCD of 90 and 1715):

$$90 = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5, \quad 1715 = 5 \cdot 7^3$$

$$\text{LCD} = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 7^3 = 2 \cdot 9 \cdot 5 \cdot 343 = 30870$$

$$P(S \mid d_{test}) \propto \frac{1}{90} = \frac{343}{30870}$$

$$P(N \mid d_{test}) \propto \frac{24}{1715} = \frac{432}{30870}$$

Since $\dfrac{432}{30870} > \dfrac{343}{30870}$, we have:

$$P(N \mid d_{test}) > P(S \mid d_{test})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hence, the test document is classified as NOT SPAM.}}$$

### Step 6 (Optional): Normalize to True Probabilities

$$P(S \mid d_{test}) = \frac{343}{343 + 432} = \frac{343}{775} \approx 0.4426$$

$$P(N \mid d_{test}) = \frac{432}{775} \approx 0.5574$$

These sum to **1** as expected.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** If you use log-probabilities (Step 4 alternative), compute $\log P(S) + \sum \log \hat{P}(w_i \mid S)$ and compare. The decision is the same, but you avoid underflow in high-dimensional problems — a common KTU question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Bayes Classification Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end data flow** of a Naive Bayes classifier — from raw input to the final class label, including the independence factorization and MAP decision block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Training Data X and Labels C] --> B[Compute Prior Probabilities Pc for each class c]
    A --> C[Compute Feature Likelihoods Pxi given c for each feature and class]
    B --> D[Store Prior Table]
    C --> E[Apply Laplace Smoothing alpha if any count equals zero]
    E --> F[Store Likelihood Table]
    D --> G[Receive New Test Instance xTest]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Posterior Score per class: Score c = Pc times product of Pxi given c]
    H --> I[Apply Argmax to select class with maximum posterior]
    I --> J[Output Predicted Class Label]
    style A fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style E fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style I fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style J fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9
```

## 4.2 Naive Bayes Independence Assumption — Structural View

This Mermaid block depicts the **factorization of the joint likelihood** under the naive independence assumption, contrasted with the **fully dependent (chain-rule) form** that Naive Bayes approximates.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph J0[Full Joint Likelihood]
        J1[P x1 x2 x3 given c]
    end
    subgraph J2[Chain Rule Expansion]
        K1[P x1 given c] --> K2[P x2 given x1 c] --> K3[P x3 given x1 x2 c]
    end
    subgraph J3[Naive Independence Assumption]
        L1[P x1 given c] --> M1[Product over i of P xi given c]
        L2[P x2 given c] --> M1
        L3[P x3 given c] --> M1
    end
    J0 --> J2 --> J3
    style M1 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#16a34a
    style K1 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207
    style K2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207
    style K3 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207
    style L1 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0284c7
    style L2 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0284c7
    style L3 fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0284c7
```
